docs.text: Containers start successfully and remain running.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Stable running containers indicate the deployment likely succeeded.
  • Immediate exit or restart loops indicate remaining config or compatibility problems.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. This changes live reverse proxy components and can temporarily interrupt ingress traffic.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Review the Compose diff and image tag changes before restarting production-facing proxy services.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: docker logs -f traefik

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Follow Traefik container logs in real time.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Streams stdout/stderr from the Traefik container continuously.

docs.title: Important parts
docs.text: • -f follows the log stream

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To verify whether the Docker provider initializes correctly after upgrade.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Startup logs showing provider loading, router discovery, and absence of API version errors.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• No provider error indicates the compatibility issue is resolved.
  • Reappearance of the API version error means the old image, wrong tag, or another incompatible component is still in play.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low risk. Read-only.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: image: traefik:v3.1

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Pin Traefik to a current major release in Compose configuration.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Tells Docker Compose which Traefik image version to deploy.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To replace an outdated Traefik version that embedded an old Docker client API.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A newer Traefik binary with current Docker provider compatibility.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Successful deployment suggests API compatibility should improve.
  • If startup still fails, review other static flags, provider settings, or whether the image actually changed.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Major version upgrades can introduce config differences.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Use a tested minor version pin and review Traefik release notes before upgrading.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: DOCKER_API_VERSION=1.44

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Force the Docker client library inside the container to negotiate with API version 1.44.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Overrides automatic API version selection through an environment variable.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: As a temporary workaround when an immediate Traefik upgrade is not possible.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The Docker provider may successfully talk to the Docker daemon if the client library can operate at that version.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Success suggests temporary compatibility can be restored.
  • Failure means the binary itself is too old or otherwise incompatible.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. This is a workaround, not a long-term fix.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Upgrade Traefik rather than relying on API version forcing.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: Likely command used: docker compose logs traefik dockersocket

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Review recent logs from the Traefik and dockersocket services together.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows log output for both containers in one view.

docs.title: Why it was likely used
docs.text: The troubleshooting relied on correlated logs from both services, which are commonly viewed this way in Compose environments.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Interleaved container logs showing the provider error and controlled shutdown sequence.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text: Useful for identifying which service emitted the first fault and which only reacted afterward.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low risk. Read-only.

docs.title: Traefik Docker Provider Fails After Docker Engine Upgrade
Summary
docs.text: Traefik stopped working correctly after a Docker Engine upgrade on the Debian Docker VM. The initial failure was traced to a Docker API version mismatch between Traefik and the Docker daemon when Traefik was using the Docker provider through docker-socket-proxy.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Debian Docker VM: debian-docker
  • Docker Engine Community
  • Traefik container
  • dockersocket container using tecnativa/docker-socket-proxy
  • Docker Compose-managed Traefik stack
  • External Docker network: traefik-proxy
  • Domain: dulynoted.cloud
  • TLS via Let’s Encrypt DNS-01 with Cloudflare
  • Dynamic Traefik config directory under /opt/docker-apps/Traefik/config/dynamic
  • Static Traefik config under /opt/docker-apps/Traefik/config/traefik.yml

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Traefik could not initialize its Docker provider after the Docker host was upgraded. This prevented label-based routing and caused dependent services such as the dashboard route to fail.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Traefik logs showed Docker provider failures:
Error response from daemon: client version 1.24 is too old. Minimum supported API version is 1.44
  • dockersocket logs showed requests such as:
GET /v1.24/version HTTP/1.1
  • Traefik exited or shut down gracefully after provider failures.
  • Label-based routes were not loaded.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Reviewed the Traefik Compose stack and confirmed Traefik was using:
--providers.docker.endpoint=tcp://dockersocket:2375
  1. Reviewed the docker-socket-proxy service definition.
  2. Confirmed the Docker Engine version on the host:
docker version
  1. Determined the host was running Docker 29.0.1 with minimum API version 1.44.
  2. Considered image refresh and cleanup of old proxy images.
  3. Reviewed Traefik static and dynamic configuration files to prepare for an upgrade.
  4. Asked whether the issue was fixed in newer Traefik releases.
  5. Determined that Traefik 3.6.x addressed the Docker 29 API compatibility issue.
  6. Upgraded Traefik from the older 3.1 line to Traefik 3.6.2.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The failure was not caused by filesystem permissions.
  • The original issue was an API compatibility problem between:
  • Docker Engine 29.0.1
  • older Traefik Docker-provider behavior using API 1.24
  • The issue was resolved by moving to Traefik 3.6.2, which negotiated against newer Docker API versions.
  • The proxy logs later showed healthy v1.51 API calls, confirming the API mismatch had been resolved.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: Traefik was upgraded to v3.6.2, replacing the earlier release line that was incompatible with Docker 29’s minimum supported API version.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Validation was based on later dockersocket log entries showing successful requests such as:

GET /v1.51/containers/json?all=1 HTTP/1.1
GET /v1.51/containers/<id>/json HTTP/1.1

These confirmed that Traefik was successfully querying Docker through the proxy using a current API version.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Keep Traefik pinned to a release compatible with the current Docker Engine.
  • Monitor Traefik release notes before future Docker major-version upgrades.
  • Remove any old workaround assumptions tied to the obsolete 1.24 API behavior.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Docker major-version upgrades can break reverse-proxy integrations even when Compose files are unchanged.
  • When Traefik Docker provider errors mention API versions, verify both the Docker daemon version and the Traefik release line before troubleshooting permissions or routing.
  • Check current logs, not just historic startup logs, before assuming an issue is still active.

docs.title: Traefik Configuration Refactor and Static/Dynamic YAML Cleanup
Summary
docs.text: The Traefik stack was reworked from a large command: block in Compose toward a file-based configuration model using a static traefik.yml and dynamic YAML files under the file provider. The goal was to simplify management, modernize for Traefik v3, and make the setup easier to debug.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Traefik stack managed with Docker Compose
  • Static config file: /opt/docker-apps/Traefik/config/traefik.yml
  • Dynamic config file provider directory: /opt/docker-apps/Traefik/config/dynamic
  • Dynamic route file name in use: routes.yml
  • Cloudflare DNS-01 for ACME
  • Dashboard routed at traefik.dulynoted.cloud

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The Traefik stack needed cleanup and modernization. There was also concern that the dynamic filename might need to be referenced explicitly.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Static configuration was split across many CLI flags.
  • The user wanted a full updated set of YAML files.
  • There was uncertainty about whether routes.yml needed to be referenced by name.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Reworked the stack conceptually so Traefik would read a static config file instead of relying mainly on CLI flags.
  2. Confirmed that the file provider was configured to load an entire directory:
providers:
  file:
    directory: /etc/traefik/dynamic
    watch: true
  1. Confirmed that the specific filename routes.yml did not matter as long as it existed in the dynamic config directory and had valid YAML.
  2. Produced full example YAML for:
  3. Traefik Compose stack
  4. static traefik.yml
  5. dynamic routes file

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Traefik file provider loads all YAML files in the configured directory.
  • The dynamic file did not need to be named homeapps.yml; routes.yml was acceptable.
  • The distinction between static config and dynamic config was important:
  • static: entrypoints, providers, API, logging, ACME
  • dynamic: routers, services, middlewares, TLS options

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The configuration model was standardized around: - Compose stack for container wiring - static traefik.yml - dynamic routes.yml

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Validation was conceptual at this stage: the file-provider path and naming behavior were confirmed, and the generated YAML aligned with Traefik v3 conventions.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Keep dynamic files logically separated if they grow large, for example:
  • routes.yml
  • middlewares.yml
  • tls.yml
  • Avoid duplicating the same setting in both CLI flags and static YAML.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Use file-based config for readability and change control.
  • File provider references like @file refer to the provider, not an individual filename.
  • Reducing config duplication makes troubleshooting much easier.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: After identifying the Docker 29 API compatibility issue, a temporary workaround was attempted by lowering Docker’s minimum API version in daemon.json so older Docker-provider clients could still connect.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Debian Docker VM
  • Docker Engine 29.0.1
  • /etc/docker/daemon.json
  • Traefik using Docker provider through dockersocket

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Traefik’s Docker provider was blocked by Docker Engine 29 rejecting older API versions.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Docker daemon reported:
minimum version 1.44
  • Traefik/provider failures continued until compatibility was addressed.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Reviewed the current Docker daemon config:
{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": { "max-size": "50m", "max-file": "3" },
  "data-root": "/var/lib/docker",
  "exec-opts": ["native.cgroupdriver=systemd"]
}
  1. Added:
"min-api-version": "1.24"
  1. Restarted Docker and restarted the Traefik stack.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The workaround was viable as a temporary bridge.
  • Once Traefik was upgraded to 3.6.2, this workaround was no longer required.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The line was added temporarily, then later removed after Traefik 3.6.2 was adopted.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: This was a transitional workaround; final validation came later when the upgraded Traefik build spoke modern API versions directly and the extra daemon setting could be removed.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Keep /etc/docker/daemon.json minimal and only retain overrides still required.
  • Re-check daemon behavior after Traefik upgrades.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• A daemon-side compatibility override can be useful temporarily, but application-side fixes are preferable.
  • Remove temporary compatibility settings after the underlying issue is resolved.

docs.title: Traefik 3.6.2 Upgrade Resolves Docker API Mismatch
Summary
docs.text: Traefik was upgraded to 3.6.2, which resolved the obsolete API-version negotiation issue with Docker 29.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Traefik 3.6.2
  • Docker Engine 29.0.1
  • dockersocket proxy
  • External Docker network: traefik-proxy

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Even after earlier troubleshooting, the stack still needed a Traefik build that could properly talk to Docker 29.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text: Earlier logs showed API failures against /v1.24/version.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Updated the Traefik image reference to:
image: traefik:v3.6.2
  1. Pulled and restarted the stack.
  2. Reviewed new logs from both traefik and dockersocket.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• New log traffic showed requests against /v1.51/....
  • dockersocket returned HTTP 200 for large numbers of /containers/.../json and /containers/json?all=1 calls.
  • This confirmed that the Traefik Docker provider was no longer using the obsolete 1.24 API.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: Traefik 3.6.2 became the target version for the homelab reverse-proxy stack.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: The following behavior validated the fix: - successful proxy logs with v1.51 - no recurrence of the original “client version 1.24 is too old” error in current logs

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Keep a note in homelab documentation that Docker 29 requires newer Traefik.
  • Review whether any daemon-side workaround remains necessary.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• When a new release claims to fix a provider-level issue, validate by checking the actual API paths in proxy logs.
  • A successful version bump often changes the failure mode, which is useful progress even if more issues remain.

docs.title: docker-socket-proxy Scope and Startup Race Troubleshooting
Summary
docs.text: Once the API mismatch was fixed, attention shifted to the relationship between Traefik and docker-socket-proxy. The proxy was being queried successfully, but Traefik logs still showed intermittent provider errors involving dockersocket.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Traefik 3.6.2
  • ghcr.io/tecnativa/docker-socket-proxy:latest or tecnativa/docker-socket-proxy
  • Docker network: traefik-proxy
  • Compose-managed Traefik stack

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Traefik intermittently logged: - DNS lookup errors for dockersocket - inability to connect to tcp://dockersocket:2375 - provider retries during startup

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Traefik logs contained:
lookup dockersocket on 127.0.0.11:53: no such host
Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at tcp://dockersocket:2375
unexpected EOF
  • At the same time, dockersocket logs showed many successful 200 responses to Traefik-originated Docker API requests.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Verified both containers were attached to the same external network.
  2. Inspected network aliases for traefik and dockersocket.
  3. Added or reviewed docker-socket-proxy scopes such as:
  4. INFO=1
  5. VERSION=1
  6. PING=1
  7. Added a known-good Compose stack with:
  8. depends_on
  9. shared traefik-proxy network
  10. explicit proxy permissions
  11. Reviewed recent logs instead of only earliest logs.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• docker inspect confirmed both containers were on traefik-proxy and had correct aliases.
  • Historic startup log lines could be misleading after the system had already recovered.
  • The proxy was successfully serving modern API requests.
  • Remaining startup errors were likely race conditions or transient during provider initialization, not the original API mismatch.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The proxy scopes were expanded to include endpoints Traefik needed, and the stack was normalized around a shared external network with dockersocket available by name.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Validation came from: - successful docker inspect network checks - successful dockersocket HTTP 200 responses - absence of the original 1.24 API error - recognition that some earlier provider errors were stale log entries

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Continue reviewing only fresh log windows during troubleshooting.
  • Keep INFO, VERSION, and PING enabled for Traefik if using docker-socket-proxy.
  • Consider whether direct socket access is preferable if proxy behavior becomes too noisy.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• docker-socket-proxy can appear healthy while historic Traefik logs still show old startup errors.
  • Always compare current container state with current logs before drawing conclusions.
  • Startup races and stale logs can look like active failures if log filtering is too broad.

docs.title: Dashboard Route Returns HTTP 500 and Cloudflare Masks Origin Behavior
Summary
docs.text: After Traefik and Docker-provider communication improved, requests to traefik.dulynoted.cloud still failed. Testing showed Cloudflare was terminating TLS at the edge and returning HTTP 500, while direct LAN testing against the Docker VM also returned HTTP 500 from Traefik itself.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Domain: traefik.dulynoted.cloud
  • Cloudflare-managed DNS
  • Traefik dashboard exposed via api@internal
  • Docker VM IP: 192.168.16.3
  • OPNsense WAN and NAT path assumed in front of the homelab

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The dashboard route failed even after Docker-provider/API issues were addressed.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
External test:

curl -kv https://traefik.dulynoted.cloud -H "Host: traefik.dulynoted.cloud"

showed: - Cloudflare anycast IPv6 address - Cloudflare edge certificate - server: cloudflare - HTTP/2 500

Internal LAN test:

curl -kv https://192.168.16.3 -H "Host: traefik.dulynoted.cloud"

showed: - TRAEFIK DEFAULT CERT - HTTP/2 500

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Confirmed the dashboard route labels existed on the Traefik container.
  2. Tested the public hostname from the Docker host.
  3. Determined the public request was terminating at Cloudflare rather than at Traefik directly.
  4. Tested directly to the Docker VM IP with the correct Host header.
  5. Confirmed the 500 was also reproducible directly against Traefik.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Cloudflare was masking the origin path by returning its own 500 while proxying.
  • Even without Cloudflare in the path, Traefik itself returned 500.
  • This proved the remaining failure was inside Traefik configuration and not purely a DNS, NAT, or WAN-forwarding issue.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The investigation moved away from DNS and toward Traefik config itself. Cloudflare remained a factor for public access path clarity, but it was no longer considered the sole cause of failure.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Internal direct-IP curl with Host: traefik.dulynoted.cloud confirmed Traefik itself was producing the 500.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Change Cloudflare records for troubleshooting to DNS only if needed during future testing.
  • Revalidate OPNsense WAN forwards for 80/443 once Traefik responds cleanly internally.
  • Review dashboard router definition independent of Docker labels.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Always test both the public hostname and the internal origin IP with the correct Host header.
  • If direct origin testing reproduces the problem, Cloudflare is not the root cause.
  • Cloudflare can obscure whether a problem is at the edge or at the origin.

docs.title: Traefik Dashboard 500 Traced to Middleware and Router Design
Summary
docs.text: The remaining HTTP 500 issue was narrowed to Traefik configuration. EntryPoint-level middleware and unfinished Organizr-related middleware references were identified as likely causes. The dashboard router was redesigned to be provided by the file provider instead of Docker labels.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Traefik 3.6.2
  • Static config file: /opt/docker-apps/Traefik/config/traefik.yml
  • Dynamic config file: /opt/docker-apps/Traefik/config/dynamic/routes.yml
  • Dashboard route: traefik.dulynoted.cloud
  • Unfinished Organizr middleware configuration not yet ready for use

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Traefik returned HTTP 500 for the dashboard even when accessed directly on the LAN.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Direct-IP test returned:
HTTP/2 500
CN=TRAEFIK DEFAULT CERT
  • EntryPoint-level middleware references existed:
middlewares:
  - securityHeaders@file
  - middlewares-rate-limit@file
  • Organizr middleware references remained active even though Organizr was not ready to use.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Reviewed the full static traefik.yml.
  2. Identified entryPoint-level middlewares as a likely place to isolate the error.
  3. Planned to temporarily comment out those entryPoint-level middlewares.
  4. Planned to move the dashboard router away from Docker labels and into the file provider.
  5. Recognized that unfinished Organizr middleware references should be disabled.
  6. User identified that the Organizr middleware YAML had not yet been commented out.
  7. Recommended commenting out Organizr references in the dynamic config.
  8. Produced a cleaned known-good stack:
  9. Compose stack
  10. static traefik.yml
  11. dynamic routes.yml
  12. Adjusted the proposed static config so entryPoint-level middlewares were disabled during debugging.
  13. Defined the dashboard route directly in routes.yml:
http:
  routers:
    traefik-api:
      rule: "Host(`traefik.dulynoted.cloud`)"
      entryPoints:
        - websecure
      service: api@internal
      tls: {}

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• A misbehaving or unavailable middleware can cause Traefik to return HTTP 500.
  • Routing the dashboard through file-provider config is simpler to debug than relying on label-based self-routing on the Traefik container.
  • Unfinished authentication integrations such as Organizr should be fully disabled until ready.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
Current working direction: - comment out unfinished Organizr middleware references - disable entryPoint-level middlewares temporarily - route the dashboard from routes.yml using api@internal - keep Traefik logging at DEBUG during the troubleshooting phase

Final operational state was not yet fully confirmed in this chat, but the likely configuration fault was identified and isolated.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Validation steps defined during the session: - restart Traefik after commenting out unfinished middleware - retest:

curl -kv https://192.168.16.3 -H "Host: traefik.dulynoted.cloud"
  • review fresh Traefik logs at debug level around request time

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Comment out or remove all Organizr-related middleware references until Organizr is implemented.
  • Re-test dashboard routing after middleware cleanup.
  • Once stable, re-enable security middlewares one at a time.
  • Once internal routing works, return Cloudflare to desired proxy mode if appropriate.
  • Store Cloudflare API token in .env and rotate any previously exposed token.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Unfinished authentication middleware should not be left active in production-like routing.
  • Debugging Traefik is easier when:
  • dashboard routing is file-based
  • middlewares are attached incrementally
  • log level is temporarily raised to DEBUG
  • A 500 from Traefik itself is a strong indicator of configuration or middleware failure, not DNS.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker version

Purpose: Confirm Docker client/server versions and supported API versions. What it does: Shows the Docker Engine client and server version, API version, and minimum API version. Why it was used: To verify whether Docker 29.0.1 was enforcing a minimum API version incompatible with older Traefik behavior. Expected result: Server output showing current API version and minimum supported version. Success/failure meaning: If the minimum API version exceeds what the client/provider uses, provider connections can fail.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker compose down

Purpose: Stop and remove the current Compose stack containers. What it does: Stops running containers and removes the Compose-managed resources for the stack. Why it was used: To restart the Traefik stack cleanly after image, config, or environment changes. Expected result: Existing traefik and dockersocket containers are removed. Success/failure meaning: A clean restart reduces ambiguity from stale containers. Risk: Low to moderate; this interrupts reverse-proxy service during the restart window.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker pull tecnativa/docker-socket-proxy:latest

Purpose: Refresh the docker-socket-proxy image. What it does: Pulls the latest image tag from the registry. Why it was used: To ensure an old cached image was not causing the Docker API problem. Expected result: New image layers are downloaded or confirmed current. Success/failure meaning: A fresh image removes “old cached image” as a variable.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker pull traefik:v3.6.2

Purpose: Upgrade Traefik to a release compatible with Docker 29. What it does: Pulls the specified Traefik image. Why it was used: To fix the Docker API compatibility issue seen with older Traefik behavior. Expected result: The 3.6.2 image is downloaded or confirmed present. Success/failure meaning: Successful pull allows Compose to recreate the container on a known-good release.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker compose up -d

Purpose: Start or recreate the stack in detached mode. What it does: Builds or recreates containers as needed and starts them in the background. Why it was used: To apply Compose, image, or config changes. Expected result: traefik and dockersocket return to running state. Success/failure meaning: If containers fail to start, log review is required.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker logs traefik | sed -n '1,80p'

Purpose: Review the earliest part of the Traefik log output. What it does: Prints the first 80 lines of container logs. Why it was used: To review startup behavior and identify initial provider failures. Expected result: Early startup messages and any first errors. Success/failure meaning: Useful for boot-time issues, but can be misleading later if old log entries are mistaken for current problems. Safer alternative: Use time filtering when troubleshooting current state.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker logs traefik --since 5m

Purpose: Review only recent Traefik log output. What it does: Shows logs generated in the last five minutes. Why it was used: To avoid confusing stale startup errors with active faults. Expected result: Current provider, routing, or middleware messages. Success/failure meaning: Best indicator of present state during active debugging.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker logs traefik --since 5m | grep -i providerName=docker || echo "no docker provider errors last 5m"

Purpose: Filter current Traefik logs for Docker-provider errors. What it does: Searches recent logs for Docker-provider entries and prints a fallback message if none are found. Why it was used: To determine whether the Docker provider was still actively failing. Expected result: Either current provider error lines or confirmation that none were seen recently. Success/failure meaning: No fresh errors suggests the provider is healthy.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker logs dockersocket --since 5m

Purpose: Review current docker-socket-proxy activity. What it does: Shows recent proxy requests and HTTP status codes. Why it was used: To confirm whether Traefik was successfully reaching the Docker API through the proxy. Expected result: Requests such as /v1.51/containers/json?all=1 returning 200. Success/failure meaning: HTTP 200 responses indicate healthy proxy/API communication.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker inspect traefik | grep -A10 'traefik.http.routers.api.rule'

Purpose: Confirm that the expected dashboard labels are present on the Traefik container. What it does: Searches the container’s inspection data for router labels. Why it was used: To verify whether the dashboard route was actually being defined by Docker labels. Expected result: Label lines such as:

traefik.http.routers.api.rule
traefik.http.routers.api.service
traefik.http.routers.api.tls

Success/failure meaning: Missing labels would explain missing routes.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker inspect traefik | grep -A5 traefik-proxy
docker inspect dockersocket | grep -A5 traefik-proxy

Purpose: Verify that both containers are attached to the same Docker network. What it does: Shows a subset of container inspect output around the traefik-proxy network section. Why it was used: To confirm name resolution and shared-network assumptions for dockersocket. Expected result: Both containers show membership in traefik-proxy with proper aliases. Success/failure meaning: If not on the same network, Traefik cannot resolve or reach dockersocket.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker network ls | grep traefik-proxy || docker network create traefik-proxy

Purpose: Ensure the external Docker network exists. What it does: Checks for the network and creates it if missing. Why it was used: The Traefik stack relied on an external shared network. Expected result: Existing network name shown or newly created network. Success/failure meaning: Missing external networks break inter-container communication across stacks.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
curl -kv https://traefik.dulynoted.cloud -H "Host: traefik.dulynoted.cloud"

Purpose: Test the public dashboard route. What it does: Sends an HTTPS request with verbose TLS and HTTP output while preserving the intended Host header. Why it was used: To determine whether the public request reached Traefik or was intercepted earlier by Cloudflare. Expected result: TLS certificate details and HTTP response headers/body. Success/failure meaning: - Cloudflare certificate/headers indicate the request is terminating at Cloudflare. - Traefik default cert indicates the request reached Traefik directly. Risk: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
curl -kv https://192.168.16.3 -H "Host: traefik.dulynoted.cloud"

Purpose: Bypass public DNS and test Traefik directly on the LAN. What it does: Connects directly to the Docker VM IP while sending the intended Host header for route matching. Why it was used: To isolate whether the problem was at Cloudflare, WAN routing, or Traefik itself. Expected result: A direct origin response from Traefik. Success/failure meaning: - If the same failure occurs directly, the problem is inside Traefik/config. - If the failure only occurs publicly, the problem is upstream of Traefik.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo cp /etc/docker/daemon.json /etc/docker/daemon.json.bak

Purpose: Back up Docker daemon configuration before editing. What it does: Copies the config file to a backup path. Why it was used: To preserve a rollback point before adding min-api-version. Expected result: Backup file created. Success/failure meaning: Backups reduce risk when editing daemon settings.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo nano /etc/docker/daemon.json

Purpose: Edit Docker daemon configuration. What it does: Opens the file in a text editor. Why it was used: To temporarily add "min-api-version": "1.24". Expected result: Updated valid JSON configuration. Success/failure meaning: Invalid JSON would prevent Docker from restarting. Risk: Moderate; bad syntax can interrupt the Docker service.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo systemctl restart docker

Purpose: Restart Docker Engine after daemon config changes. What it does: Restarts the Docker service. Why it was used: To apply daemon.json changes. Expected result: Docker comes back up cleanly. Success/failure meaning: Failure indicates invalid config or Docker service issues. Risk: Moderate; all containers on the host are affected by a Docker service restart.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
grep -Rni "organizr" /opt/docker-apps/Traefik/config/dynamic || echo "No remaining Organizr refs"

Purpose: Find active Organizr references in dynamic Traefik config files. What it does: Recursively searches for the string organizr. Why it was used: To ensure unfinished Organizr middleware or router references were not still active. Expected result: File/line matches or a message indicating none remain. Success/failure meaning: Active references can break routing if the middleware or service is not ready.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo mv organizr.yml organizr.yml.disabled

Likely command used

Purpose: Disable a dynamic Traefik file without deleting it. What it does: Renames the file so the file provider no longer loads it as YAML. Why it was suggested: To completely disable unfinished Organizr config. Expected result: Traefik ignores the renamed file. Success/failure meaning: Useful for safely parking incomplete config during troubleshooting.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker exec -it traefik sh

Purpose: Enter the Traefik container for live troubleshooting. What it does: Starts an interactive shell inside the running container. Why it was suggested: To test DNS resolution and HTTP access to dockersocket from inside the Traefik container. Expected result: Shell prompt inside the container. Success/failure meaning: In-container tests help distinguish container-network issues from host-network issues. Risk: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ping -c 1 dockersocket

Likely command used

Purpose: Test name resolution and basic reachability to the docker-socket-proxy container. What it does: Sends one ICMP echo request to dockersocket. Why it was suggested: To confirm Docker DNS resolution inside the Traefik container. Expected result: A resolved IP and a reply. Success/failure meaning: Failure would support a Docker-network or alias problem.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
curl http://dockersocket:2375/version

Likely command used

Purpose: Test the Docker API version endpoint through the proxy. What it does: Sends a direct HTTP request to the proxy’s /version endpoint. Why it was suggested: To confirm that the proxy allowed the API group Traefik needed. Expected result: JSON version information from the Docker daemon. Success/failure meaning: Failure indicates proxy-scope or connectivity issues.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ls -l /etc/docker/daemon.json

Purpose: Confirm whether Docker daemon configuration file exists. What it does: Lists file metadata. Why it was used: Before editing daemon config for compatibility testing. Expected result: File path, ownership, and permissions. Success/failure meaning: Missing file means the daemon may still be using defaults.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
cd /opt/docker-apps/Traefik/config/dynamic
ls

Purpose: Inspect dynamic Traefik config files. What it does: Lists files in the dynamic config directory. Why it was used: To identify the Organizr YAML file and confirm file naming such as routes.yml. Expected result: List of active dynamic config files. Success/failure meaning: Helps identify incomplete or conflicting config files.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker logs traefik --tail 80

Purpose: Review the most recent block of Traefik logs. What it does: Prints the last 80 lines. Why it was used: To inspect the latest behavior after config edits and restarts. Expected result: Current startup/provider/request messages. Success/failure meaning: Useful when you need recent data but not a time filter.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker compose up -d traefik

Purpose: Recreate or restart only the Traefik service. What it does: Applies service changes to just the Traefik container. Why it was used: To reload Traefik after YAML edits without unnecessarily restarting unrelated services. Expected result: Traefik container recreated or restarted. Success/failure meaning: Faster iteration during troubleshooting.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker compose up -d dockersocket

Purpose: Recreate or restart only the docker-socket-proxy service. What it does: Applies service changes to only the proxy container. Why it was used: To refresh proxy scopes or image state without cycling the whole stack. Expected result: Proxy container recreated or restarted. Success/failure meaning: Useful for targeted debugging.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker system prune

Likely command considered

Purpose: Clean up unused Docker resources. What it does: Removes stopped containers, dangling images, unused networks, and optionally more. Why it was discussed: As a way to reduce stale image/container confusion during image-purge troubleshooting. Expected result: Unused resources removed. Success/failure meaning: Can simplify image-state troubleshooting. Risk: Moderate; review carefully before running in a homelab with multiple stacks. Safer alternative: Remove only specific images or containers with docker rmi and docker rm.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker images | grep -E 'tecnativa|traefik'

Purpose: List relevant local images. What it does: Filters image listing for Traefik and docker-socket-proxy images. Why it was used: To identify stale or duplicate images during purge/update work. Expected result: Matching image tags and IDs. Success/failure meaning: Helps confirm which image versions are actually present on the host.

docs.title: Create External Docker Network for Traefik
Summary
docs.text: A Docker network named traefik-proxy was created for use as a shared external network between Traefik and application stacks. The goal was to establish a standard network that multiple Docker Compose projects could attach to so Traefik could reach backend containers across separate stacks.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Docker host in the homelab
  • Docker CLI
  • Docker Compose-based application stacks
  • Traefik reverse proxy
  • External shared Docker bridge network: traefik-proxy

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: A dedicated shared Docker network was needed so Traefik and other containers could communicate consistently across Compose projects.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• No explicit error was recorded in this chat.
  • The requirement was stated as a configuration task: create a Docker network called traefik-proxy using the CLI.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Created the Docker network from the CLI.
docker network create traefik-proxy

Purpose: Create a shared Docker bridge network named traefik-proxy.

  1. Noted the explicit driver form as an equivalent option.
docker network create --driver bridge traefik-proxy

Purpose: Create the same network while explicitly specifying the standard single-host bridge driver.

  1. Documented how Docker Compose stacks should reference the network as an external network.
networks:
  traefik-proxy:
    external: true

Purpose: Allow Compose-managed services to attach to the pre-existing shared network instead of trying to create their own isolated network.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• docker network create traefik-proxy is sufficient for creating the network in normal single-host Docker setups.
  • Explicitly setting --driver bridge is optional in this case because bridge is the expected driver for a standard local Docker network.
  • Marking the network as external: true in Compose is important when multiple stacks need to join the same pre-created network.
  • This pattern is useful for Traefik because it often runs in one stack while proxied applications run in others.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
The required Docker network creation command was identified and documented. The final operational command was:

docker network create traefik-proxy

Current status: the runbook step is defined and ready to execute on the Docker host.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Success would be confirmed by: - the command completing without error - the network appearing in Docker's network list - Traefik and application containers being able to attach to traefik-proxy - Compose stacks successfully using external: true for that network

Likely validation command:

docker network ls

Purpose: Verify that traefik-proxy exists.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Verify that Traefik is attached to traefik-proxy
  • Ensure all proxied app stacks join the same external network
  • Standardize Compose files so they all reference the same network name
  • Optionally inspect the network after creation to confirm attached containers and subnet assignment
  • Document this as a reusable baseline step in the homelab reverse proxy setup guide

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• A shared external Docker network is a clean way to connect Traefik to containers spread across multiple Compose projects.
  • Pre-creating the network avoids duplicate per-stack networks that Traefik cannot automatically use across projects.
  • Keeping the network name standardized reduces routing and service discovery mistakes in homelab deployments.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker network create traefik-proxy

What it does Creates a Docker network named traefik-proxy.

Why it was used A shared network was needed so Traefik and other containers from separate Compose projects could communicate on the same Docker network.

Important arguments - network create: Tells Docker to create a new network. - traefik-proxy: The name assigned to the network.

Expected result Docker creates the network and returns either the network ID or a success response.

What success indicates The Docker host now has a network named traefik-proxy available for container attachment.

What failure would indicate - The network may already exist - Docker may not be running - The user may lack permission to access the Docker socket

Risk level Low risk.

Safer alternative Check whether the network already exists before creating it:

docker network ls

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker network create --driver bridge traefik-proxy

What it does Creates the traefik-proxy network and explicitly sets the driver to bridge.

Why it was used This makes the network type explicit. In most single-host Docker homelab setups, this is the standard network driver for inter-container communication.

Important arguments - --driver bridge: Uses Docker's local bridge networking driver.

Expected result A bridge network named traefik-proxy is created.

What success indicates The network exists and uses the standard local bridge model.

What failure would indicate - Same issues as above - Invalid driver selection, if mistyped

Risk level Low risk.

Homelab relevance For Docker and Traefik, bridge networks are the standard way to let reverse proxy containers reach backend app containers on the same host.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
networks:
  traefik-proxy:
    external: true

What it does This is a Docker Compose network definition telling Compose to use an already existing Docker network rather than create a new one.

Why it was used Traefik commonly runs in one Compose stack while apps run in others. Using external: true lets all those stacks join the same network.

Important fields - traefik-proxy: The network name referenced by the Compose project. - external: true: Tells Compose not to create the network and instead attach to an existing one.

Expected result When the stack starts, its containers attach to the pre-created traefik-proxy network.

What success indicates The stack is using the shared network correctly, which supports Traefik routing across stacks.

What failure would indicate If the network does not already exist, Compose will fail when bringing the stack up.

Risk level Low risk.

Safer note Create the network first before deploying the Compose stack.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker network ls

Likely command used

What it does Lists Docker networks on the host.

Why it was likely used This is the most direct way to verify that traefik-proxy was created successfully.

Expected result A network list that includes traefik-proxy.

What success indicates The network exists and is available for use by Traefik and app containers.

What failure would indicate If traefik-proxy is absent, the creation step may not have been run successfully.

Risk level Low risk.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker network inspect traefik-proxy

Likely command used

What it does Shows detailed information about the traefik-proxy network, including driver, subnet, gateway, and attached containers.

Why it was likely useful This is a strong validation step after creation, especially in a Traefik homelab where network attachment directly affects proxy routing.

Expected result Detailed JSON output describing the network.

What success indicates The network exists and can be inspected; attached containers can also be verified here.

What failure would indicate The named network does not exist or Docker is not responding.

Risk level Low risk.

Homelab relevance Useful when troubleshooting Traefik routing, label-based discovery, or cross-stack connectivity issues.

docs.title: VM 100 Boot Failure, Docker Data Disk Recovery, and Cloud-Init Hardening
Summary
docs.text:
This work session focused on recovering Proxmox VM 100 (debian-docker) after a boot failure caused by duplicate mount definitions and cloud-init drift, restoring Docker storage to the correct data disk, repairing filesystem corruption on the Docker disk, and hardening the VM’s cloud-init configuration so Docker only starts when the intended Docker data disk is mounted.

The session started as a boot and console recovery problem, expanded into mount troubleshooting, then ended with a stable Docker recovery and a hardened cloud-init design.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Platform: Proxmox VE
  • Node: mainframe
  • VM: 100 (debian-docker)
  • Helper VM: cloned from template 9000, used as a repair VM
  • Guest OS: Debian cloud image-based VM
  • Storage:
  • scsi0: cephpool:vm-100-disk-0 — OS disk, 50G
  • scsi1: cephpool:vm-100-disk-1 — Docker/data disk, 150G
  • ide2: cephpool:vm-100-cloudinit — cloud-init seed ISO
  • Docker data path: /var/lib/docker
  • App paths: /opt/compose, /opt/docker-apps
  • Remote mounts:
  • //192.168.16.21/Media → /srv/remotemount/NAS
  • //192.168.16.22/Public → /srv/remotemount/wontonsoup
  • Container stack: Docker Engine, Docker Compose, Dockge, Traefik, Gluetun-related containers, multiple app web GUIs
  • Relevant labels / IDs:
  • Docker data disk label: docker-data
  • Docker data disk UUID: 6ce44029-6b4b-4fe4-a78e-bec4402a0444
  • Cloud-init datasource: NoCloud from /dev/sr0

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: VM 100 became unbootable after old and new cloud-init mount logic overlapped, producing duplicate /etc/fstab entries and incorrect boot-time mount behavior. After boot recovery, Docker storage mounts became inconsistent, and /var/lib/docker and its bind mounts temporarily attached to the wrong backing filesystem. The Docker data disk then required offline filesystem repair.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Boot dropped into emergency mode
  • systemd errors for duplicate mount units:
  • Failed to create unit file ... Duplicate entry in /etc/fstab
  • Root account unavailable in emergency shell:
  • Cannot open access to console, the root account is locked
  • initramfs reported:
  • root=PARTUUID=... does not exist
  • Cloud-init repeatedly processed NoCloud seed data from /dev/sr0
  • /var/lib/docker mount failed earlier in boot:
  • Dependency failed for var-lib-docker.mount
  • Failed to start systemd-fsck@dev-disk-by-label-docker-data.service
  • Docker later showed as inactive until the data disk mount was corrected
  • fsck initially refused to run because /dev/sdb was still mounted
  • /opt/compose and /opt/docker-apps were observed on /dev/sda1 instead of the Docker data disk
  • cd /opt/compose/dockge failed with:
  • No such device
  • sysctl was missing in the guest at one point:
  • sysctl: command not found

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Reviewed the VM config on Proxmox and identified the VM disk layout.
  2. Confirmed that VM 100 used:
  3. scsi0 as OS disk
  4. scsi1 as Docker/data disk
  5. ide2 as cloud-init ISO
  6. Investigated boot failure messages and identified duplicate /opt/docker-apps and /opt/compose mount definitions in /etc/fstab.
  7. Determined that an older cloud-init YAML had likely been applied on top of a newer configuration, causing duplicate mount lines and boot instability.
  8. Created and used a helper VM from template 9000.
  9. Attached VM 100’s OS disk to the helper VM and mounted it for offline repair.
  10. Edited VM 100’s /etc/fstab from the helper VM:
  11. Commented duplicate /opt/docker-apps entries
  12. Commented duplicate /opt/compose entries
  13. Corrected malformed lines
  14. Replaced stale PARTUUID root references with working UUID-based mount references
  15. Detached the repaired OS disk from the helper VM and rebooted VM 100.
  16. Re-applied and reviewed cloud-init behavior, including hostname and mount handling.
  17. Observed that hostname drifted to helper-vm, confirming cloud-init and/or stale template state was being re-applied.
  18. Updated the cloud-init design so it would manage mounts consistently and own the hostname configuration.
  19. Logged into VM 100 successfully and performed post-recovery verification.
  20. Inspected mount state and discovered:
    • /var/lib/docker was expected on the Docker disk
    • /opt/compose and /opt/docker-apps had temporarily latched onto root disk paths when the correct Docker mount was absent
  21. Verified Docker mount failures and then corrected the Docker data path.
  22. Stopped Docker and containerd to free /var/lib/docker.
  23. Unmounted all child mountpoints and bind mounts under /var/lib/docker.
  24. Fully unmounted /dev/sdb.
  25. Ran an offline filesystem repair on the Docker data disk.
  26. fsck repaired:
    • orphaned inode list corruption
    • block bitmap mismatches
    • inode bitmap mismatches
    • free block and free inode count inconsistencies
  27. Remounted /var/lib/docker from the correct Docker disk and remounted bind targets for /opt/compose and /opt/docker-apps.
  28. Restarted containerd and Docker successfully.
  29. Verified Docker came back up with the correct root:
    • /var/lib/docker
    • overlay2
    • systemd cgroup driver
  30. Observed that 23 containers were active after recovery.
  31. Cleaned up the mistaken dockge directory that had been created on the wrong backing filesystem during the mount confusion.
  32. Hardened the cloud-init YAML to pin Docker storage to the intended disk and to verify the mount before Docker starts.
  33. Saved the hardened cloud-init design for future reuse.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The original boot failure was caused by duplicate mount definitions in /etc/fstab, likely from applying an older cloud-init mount configuration over a newer one.
  • The PARTUUID boot issue was separate from the Docker mount issue, but it contributed to boot instability.
  • Cloud-init was repeatedly processing NoCloud seed data from /dev/sr0, which confirmed the VM was still tied to its Proxmox cloud-init ISO.
  • The Docker data disk was a single ext4 filesystem directly on /dev/sdb, not on a partition like /dev/sdb1.
  • /var/lib/docker must be mounted from the dedicated Docker disk before bind mounts for /opt/compose and /opt/docker-apps are triggered.
  • Because the bind targets were configured as automounts, they were able to bind against the wrong source path on /dev/sda1 when /var/lib/docker on /dev/sdb was not yet mounted.
  • Docker itself was healthy once the disk mount was corrected; the real blocker was storage mount state.
  • The Docker data disk had real ext4 corruption, not just a mount ordering problem. Offline fsck was necessary and successfully repaired it.
  • The working data disk identity was:
  • Label: docker-data
  • UUID: 6ce44029-6b4b-4fe4-a78e-bec4402a0444
  • Docker was ultimately confirmed healthy with:
  • Docker Root Dir: /var/lib/docker
  • Driver: overlay2
  • Cgroup Driver: systemd

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
The recovery was completed in stages:

  1. Boot recovery
  2. Duplicate mount lines in /etc/fstab were commented out.
  3. The root disk was repaired offline using a helper VM.

  4. Boot configuration was normalized enough to get the guest back online.

  5. Docker mount recovery

  6. /var/lib/docker was remounted from the correct disk (/dev/sdb, label docker-data).
  7. /opt/compose and /opt/docker-apps were rebound to the correct directories on that disk.

  8. A mistaken Dockge directory created on the wrong backing filesystem was removed.

  9. Filesystem repair

  10. /dev/sdb was fully unmounted.

  11. Offline fsck -f -y /dev/sdb repaired ext4 corruption and metadata inconsistencies.

  12. Cloud-init hardening

  13. Cloud-init was updated so Docker storage mounts by the exact UUID of the intended data disk.
  14. A Docker mount guard service was added to verify /var/lib/docker is mounted from that exact device before Docker starts.
  15. Docker service override logic was updated to wait on the required mounts and the verification service.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Success was confirmed by all of the following: - VM 100 booted and became interactive again. - /var/lib/docker mounted from the correct disk: - /dev/sdb - Bind mounts resolved correctly: - /opt/compose → /dev/sdb[/compose] - /opt/docker-apps → /dev/sdb[/appdata] - Docker service became active and running. - docker info showed: - Root: /var/lib/docker - Driver: overlay2 - Cgroup: systemd - docker system df showed images, containers, and volumes were present. - Docker reported 23 active containers after recovery. - Cloud-init analysis showed user-data was being read from the NoCloud datasource on the Proxmox cloud-init ISO. - The hardened YAML was finalized and saved for reuse.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Reboot VM 100 once more and verify that:
  • /var/lib/docker mounts automatically from the correct UUID
  • /opt/compose and /opt/docker-apps resolve to the Docker data disk after boot
  • Docker starts without manual intervention
  • Confirm all app web GUIs are reachable after a clean reboot.
  • Verify Dockge’s compose directory and UI state after the mount correction.
  • Consider removing discard from the Docker data disk mount and relying on fstrim.timer if desired for SSD/virt trim strategy consistency.
  • Confirm all important containers use restart: unless-stopped or equivalent restart policies.
  • Keep an eye on ext4 errors or repeated fsck findings in case the Docker data disk or host experienced an unclean shutdown or storage fault.
  • Optionally install or configure additional disk health monitoring.
  • Review whether bind mounts for /opt/compose and /opt/docker-apps should remain automounts or become plain binds to reduce mount-order surprises.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Applying old cloud-init mount logic on top of newer config can silently create duplicate fstab entries and break boot.
  • A helper VM is a clean way to repair a broken guest offline when console access is limited.
  • systemd automount bind targets can bind against the wrong underlying filesystem if the intended parent mount is missing when the automount first fires.
  • For important storage like Docker data, mounting by UUID is safer than relying on label or device-name detection alone.
  • Docker startup should be explicitly guarded so it cannot come up on the wrong storage path.
  • Filesystem repair on active Docker storage must be done offline after fully stopping Docker, containerd, and all subordinate mounts.
  • Clean separation between OS disk, Docker disk, and cloud-init ownership makes recovery much easier.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: cat /etc/pve/qemu-server/100.conf

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Review the exact Proxmox VM configuration for VM 100.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows VM hardware, disks, cloud-init, boot settings, CPU, memory, and device mapping.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To confirm which disk was the OS disk, which was the Docker/data disk, and how cloud-init was attached.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A config showing scsi0, scsi1, and ide2 roles.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: VM layout is visible and can be reasoned about.
  • Failure: wrong VM ID, missing config, or Proxmox metadata issue.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: For Proxmox, this is the authoritative per-VM config and is critical for storage recovery and disk attachment work.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: qm clone 9000 101 --name helper-repair

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Create a helper VM from template 9000.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Clones a template into a new working VM that can be used to mount and repair another VM’s disks offline.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To create a rescue/helper VM for offline disk work.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A new VM ID 101 named helper-repair.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: helper VM is available for offline disk work.
  • Failure: clone problem, storage issue, or template not available.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: A helper VM is a common Proxmox recovery technique for repairing broken guest filesystems and configs.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: qm set 101 --scsi2 cephpool:vm-100-disk-0

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Attach VM 100’s OS disk to the helper VM.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Adds the existing Ceph-backed disk as an extra SCSI disk to the helper VM.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To mount and edit VM 100’s OS disk offline.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The helper VM sees VM 100’s disk as a new block device.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: disk becomes accessible for repair work.
  • Failure: disk path mismatch, storage issue, or VM config issue.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Attaching a disk to a helper VM while the original VM is still running is risky and can corrupt data.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Always shut down the original VM before re-attaching its disk elsewhere.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: In Proxmox with Ceph-backed volumes, this is a standard disk rescue workflow.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: lsblk -fp

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Inspect disks, filesystems, labels, UUIDs, and mountpoints.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Lists block devices in full path form with filesystem metadata.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To identify which guest disks were mounted where and whether Docker data was on the correct disk.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Block devices like /dev/sda, /dev/sdb, filesystem labels, and mountpoints.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: storage state is visible.
  • Failure: device enumeration issue or missing tools.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: Useful across Linux, Proxmox guests, Docker hosts, and recovery sessions.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/repair

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Mount VM 100’s root filesystem from the helper VM.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Mounts the attached disk’s filesystem to a temporary location for offline editing.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To edit /etc/fstab and other files on VM 100 while it was powered off.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Mounted guest filesystem visible under /mnt/repair.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: disk is accessible and repair can proceed.
  • Failure: wrong partition, wrong filesystem type, or corruption.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Mounting read-write changes the target guest filesystem. That was necessary here.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo sed -i '/[[:space:]]\/opt\/docker-apps[[:space:]]/ s/^/# /' /mnt/repair/etc/fstab

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Comment duplicate /opt/docker-apps mount lines in fstab.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Finds matching lines and comments them out.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To stop systemd from generating duplicate mount units and booting into emergency mode.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Duplicate lines become commented and no longer apply.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: one source of truth remains for the mount.
  • Failure: wrong pattern, wrong file, or no matching line.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Editing fstab incorrectly can make the system unbootable.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo sed -i '/[[:space:]]\/opt\/compose[[:space:]]/ s/^/# /' /mnt/repair/etc/fstab

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Comment duplicate /opt/compose mount lines in fstab.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Disables all matching active lines for that mountpoint.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To remove duplicate mount definitions that caused boot failure.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Only the intended mount mechanism remains active.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: duplicate systemd mount generation stops.
  • Failure: duplicate lines remain or wrong lines are changed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo mount -L docker-data /var/lib/docker

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Mount the Docker data disk by filesystem label.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Mounts whichever ext4 filesystem has label docker-data at /var/lib/docker.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To reattach Docker storage to the intended dedicated disk after mount drift.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: /var/lib/docker becomes backed by /dev/sdb.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: Docker root points to the correct disk.
  • Failure: wrong label, missing disk, or filesystem issue.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: If the wrong filesystem has that label, Docker could start on the wrong disk.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Mount by UUID instead of label.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: findmnt /var/lib/docker

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Confirm the current backing source for /var/lib/docker.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows the exact device or filesystem mounted at that path.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To verify whether Docker storage was on /dev/sdb or mistakenly on the root disk.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: SOURCE=/dev/sdb once corrected.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: mount source is clearly identified.
  • Failure: mount missing, path not mounted, or wrong source.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: findmnt /opt/compose

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Confirm the source backing /opt/compose.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows the bind or automount backing that path.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To detect when /opt/compose had latched onto the wrong source path on /dev/sda1.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Correct source should ultimately be /dev/sdb[/compose].

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: bind target is correctly attached to Docker data disk.
  • Failure: source drift or missing mount.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: findmnt /opt/docker-apps

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Confirm the source backing /opt/docker-apps.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows the active mount source for that bind target.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To ensure Docker app data was being pulled from the intended storage.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Correct source should be /dev/sdb[/appdata].

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: appdata bind is correct.
  • Failure: appdata bind is using the wrong filesystem.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo systemctl stop docker docker.socket containerd

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Stop Docker and container runtime services before storage work.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Stops Docker, the Docker socket activation unit, and containerd.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To release mounts and overlay usage so /var/lib/docker could be unmounted and repaired safely.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Runtime stack stops cleanly.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: data disk can be unmounted.
  • Failure: active containers or systemd restart behavior may still hold the mount.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Stopping these services interrupts all running containers.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: On a Docker host, this is the correct first step before filesystem maintenance on Docker storage.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo pkill -9 -f 'containerd-shim'

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Force-stop lingering containerd shim processes.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Kills processes matching the containerd shim process name.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To free mount references that prevented unmounting /var/lib/docker.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Shim processes exit immediately.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: mountpoints can be released.
  • Failure: other processes still hold the filesystem.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Force-killing runtime processes is disruptive and can leave temporary inconsistencies. It was justified here because the disk required offline repair.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: mount | awk '$3 ~ /^\/var\/lib\/docker(\/|$)/ {print $3}' | sort -r | xargs -r -n1 sudo umount

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Unmount child mountpoints under /var/lib/docker from deepest to shallowest.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Finds all mounts rooted under /var/lib/docker, sorts them in reverse path order, and unmounts them.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Unmounting parent mounts first fails if child mounts still exist.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: All subordinate Docker mountpoints disappear.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: the main Docker mount can be unmounted.
  • Failure: some process still holds a mountpoint open.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Unmounting live Docker storage while containers are active would be dangerous. Runtime processes were stopped first.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: mount | awk '$1=="/dev/sdb"{print $3}' | sort -r | xargs -r -n1 sudo umount

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Unmount every mountpoint backed by /dev/sdb.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Finds all current mount targets using /dev/sdb and unmounts them.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To fully detach the Docker data disk before running fsck.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: findmnt /dev/sdb returns nothing afterward.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: the disk is offline and safe to check.
  • Failure: one or more mountpoints still hold the device.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo fsck -f -y /dev/sdb

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Run an offline ext filesystem repair on the Docker data disk.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Forces a full filesystem check and automatically answers yes to repair prompts.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: The disk showed corruption symptoms and had failed boot-time fsck.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Filesystem metadata is repaired and the tool reports any fixes performed.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: corruption repaired and filesystem ready for remount.
  • Failure: serious storage damage or inability to complete repairs.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: fsck -y makes automatic modifications. It should only be run on an unmounted filesystem or in a properly offline recovery scenario.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: Critical for ext4-backed Docker data disks after unclean shutdowns or mount inconsistencies.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo mount -a

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Mount all filesystems from /etc/fstab.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Processes the current fstab and mounts anything not yet mounted.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To restore all standard mounts after repair and config changes.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Required local and automount-backed filesystems come online without errors.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: fstab is valid and mount dependencies resolve.
  • Failure: syntax errors, missing devices, or incorrect mount options.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo systemctl start containerd docker

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Restart the container runtime stack after storage recovery.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Starts containerd and Docker services.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To bring application containers back online after the Docker disk was repaired and remounted.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Docker daemon becomes active and loads containers from the correct storage root.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: Docker storage path is healthy and accessible.
  • Failure: mount path still wrong, daemon config issue, or container runtime problem.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: docker info --format 'Root: {{.DockerRootDir}}  Driver: {{.Driver}}  Cgroup: {{.CgroupDriver}}'

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Validate Docker’s core runtime state.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Prints Docker’s root directory, storage driver, and cgroup driver.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To confirm Docker was using the correct storage root after recovery.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Root: /var/lib/docker Driver: overlay2 Cgroup: systemd

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: Docker is healthy and aligned with the intended configuration.
  • Failure: wrong root path, wrong driver, or broken daemon state.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: Useful for verifying Docker host correctness after storage or daemon changes.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: docker system df

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Inspect Docker object usage.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows how much space images, containers, volumes, and build cache consume.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To confirm Docker had recovered its expected state and still saw its stored objects.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Nonzero image/container counts and sizes.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: Docker metadata and storage survived recovery.
  • Failure: missing images, missing containers, or wrong Docker root.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: docker ps --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}'

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: List currently running containers and published ports.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows names, states, and exposed ports of active containers.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To confirm that application containers came back after Docker recovery.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A list of running containers with statuses such as Up.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: app stack is active again.
  • Failure: Docker is running but containers did not restart.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/rootdisk
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/rootdisk
sudo rm -rf /mnt/rootdisk/var/lib/docker/compose/dockge
sudo umount /mnt/rootdisk

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Remove the accidental Dockge directory created on the wrong backing filesystem.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Temporarily mounts the root disk, deletes the mistaken directory path, and unmounts it.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: A Dockge folder had been created while /opt/compose was bound to the wrong source on the root disk.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The stray directory is removed from the wrong filesystem.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: cleanup completed without touching the real Docker disk.
  • Failure: wrong path, wrong disk, or mount issue.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Deleting the wrong path here could remove wanted data. Path verification is important.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: qm set 101 --delete scsi2

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Detach VM 100’s disk from the helper VM.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Removes the extra attached disk from helper VM 101’s config.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To return the disk to normal ownership after offline repair.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The helper VM no longer sees VM 100’s disk.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: disk safely detached.
  • Failure: wrong bus slot or config mismatch.

docs.title: Important note
docs.text: This detaches the disk from the helper VM config; it does not delete the disk from storage.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: Important distinction in Proxmox: config detach is not storage deletion.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: cloud-init analyze show

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Review cloud-init stage execution history and timing.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows what cloud-init stages ran, how long they took, and whether modules previously ran or ran successfully.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To confirm that NoCloud user-data was being read from /dev/sr0 and that cloud-init had applied configuration.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Boot records showing datasource detection and module execution.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: cloud-init is active and consuming the expected data source.
  • Failure: cloud-init disabled, broken datasource, or missing tools.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: Very useful when Proxmox cloud-init snippets are not behaving as expected.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: qm cloudinit update 100

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Regenerate the Proxmox cloud-init seed ISO for VM 100.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Updates the cloud-init ISO contents attached to the VM based on current Proxmox config and custom snippets.

docs.title: Why it was used or implied
docs.text: Necessary when cloud-init YAML changes and the updated config must be fed into the VM.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The VM sees refreshed NoCloud metadata and user-data on next boot.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: cloud-init changes will apply on next guest initialization.
  • Failure: snippet path, storage, or config issue.

docs.title: Homelab relevance
docs.text: Core to Proxmox cloud-init workflows.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: qm cloudinit dump 100 user

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Inspect the effective user-data Proxmox is providing to the VM.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Prints the rendered cloud-init user-data for the VM.

docs.title: Why it was used or implied
docs.text: To verify that the intended hardened YAML was actually what Proxmox would deliver.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Rendered YAML reflecting the current cloud-init design.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: user-data path and rendering are correct.
  • Failure: old snippet still referenced or config not updated.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo cloud-init clean --logs --seed

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Force cloud-init to treat the next boot as fresh initialization.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Removes cached state, logs, and seed tracking so cloud-init reruns setup.

docs.title: Why it was used or implied
docs.text: Needed when reapplying updated cloud-init config to an already-provisioned VM.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Cloud-init reruns relevant stages on next boot.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: hostname, mounts, and config changes can re-apply.
  • Failure: cloud-init version limitation or package issue.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Re-running cloud-init can reapply config in disruptive ways if the YAML is not carefully controlled.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo systemctl enable docker

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Ensure Docker starts on boot.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Enables the Docker service unit in systemd.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To make Docker and its containers recover automatically after reboot.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Docker is enabled for future boots.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: service links are created.
  • Failure: unit not found or systemd issue.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo systemctl restart docker

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Restart Docker after mount or config changes.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Stops and starts the Docker service.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Needed after storage recovery, daemon config changes, or service override updates.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Docker restarts cleanly and reconnects to the correct storage.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: Docker state stabilizes on the corrected mount.
  • Failure: mount issue persists or daemon config is invalid.

docs.title: VM 110 Provisioning Alignment, Guest Agent Check, IP Conflict Discussion, and Docker Appdata Permissions
Summary
docs.text: This work session focused on reusing the configuration pattern from Proxmox VM 100 for a new VM 110, confirming the correct CPU model, reapplying the cloud-init custom snippet configuration, checking QEMU Guest Agent status, discussing the behavior of duplicate IP addresses on the same network, and standardizing ownership and permissions for Docker appdata under /opt/docker-apps. The session ended with a practical permissions plan for appdata used by Docker services such as Traefik and Dockge.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Hypervisor: Proxmox VE
  • VMs involved:
  • VM 100: existing Debian Docker VM used as reference
  • VM 110: new VM being aligned to the same pattern
  • Guest OS context: Debian-based Docker VM
  • Cloud-init snippet storage: snips (CephFS-backed snippet storage)
  • Docker appdata path: /opt/docker-apps
  • Compose path context: /opt/compose
  • Containers / app folders mentioned: Traefik, Dockge, Radarr, Sonarr, SABnzbd, qBittorrent, Plex, Prowlarr, Lidarr, Syncthing, Vaultwarden, TubeArchivist, Audiobookshelf, and many others under /opt/docker-apps
  • Networking context: Proxmox VM networking on a shared LAN / bridge
  • Remote file management tool: WinSCP
  • Relevant Proxmox feature: QEMU Guest Agent
  • Relevant reverse proxy component: Traefik with acme.json
