  • Driver: e1000e
  • Network config file: /etc/network/interfaces

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The Intel onboard NIC on pve1 was hanging under load and causing host network loss and downstream Ceph disconnects.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Repeated:
  • e1000e ... Detected Hardware Unit Hang
  • Followed by:
  • libceph ... socket closed
  • Host and VM appeared down or frozen from the network.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Installed ethtool.
  2. Disabled TSO/GSO/GRO offloads live on eno1.
  3. Disabled TSO/GSO/GRO offloads live on vmbr0.
  4. Verified offload state using ethtool -k.
  5. Edited /etc/network/interfaces to add persistent post-up commands.
  6. Reloaded networking with ifreload -a.
  7. Re-verified offload settings after reload.

Important commands used:

apt-get install -y ethtool

Purpose: install NIC feature tuning tool.

ethtool -K eno1 tso off gso off gro off

Purpose: disable problematic offloads on the physical NIC.

ethtool -K vmbr0 tso off gso off gro off

Purpose: apply the same mitigation at the bridge layer where supported.

ethtool -k eno1 | egrep 'tso|gso|gro'

Purpose: verify the physical NIC’s offload state.

ethtool -k vmbr0 | egrep 'tso|gso|gro'

Purpose: inspect bridge feature state after the change.

nano /etc/network/interfaces

Purpose: persist the workaround across reboot.

ifreload -a

Purpose: apply the network config without a full reboot.

Persisted configuration:

auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

auto eno1
iface eno1 inet manual
    post-up /sbin/ethtool -K eno1 tso off gso off gro off

auto vmbr0
iface vmbr0 inet static
        address 192.168.16.12/24
        gateway 192.168.16.1
        bridge-ports eno1
        bridge-stp off
        bridge-fd 0
        post-up /sbin/ethtool -K vmbr0 tso off gso off gro off

iface wlp0s20f3 inet manual

source /etc/network/interfaces.d/*

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• eno1 successfully showed the relevant offload features disabled.
  • vmbr0 showed mixed bridge-specific behavior, which is normal; the key mitigation is on the physical NIC.
  • The mitigation fits a known Intel NUC / e1000e problem pattern under Linux and bursty network load.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The e1000e offload workaround was applied successfully on pve1 and made persistent.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Live ethtool changes succeeded.
  • Verification output showed the relevant offload features disabled on eno1.
  • After about a day, the node appeared stable with most containers running.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Apply the same workaround to other Intel NUC8i7BEH nodes using the same NIC/driver path.
  • Keep checking for:
  • e1000e hardware hangs
  • Ceph socket closures
  • Continue monitoring temperatures because thermal spikes were also confirmed earlier.
  • Consider BIOS updates and cooling cleanup on all NUC nodes.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Intel NUC onboard e1000e NIC instability can destabilize an entire Proxmox/Ceph node.
  • Disabling TSO/GSO/GRO is a practical and low-risk mitigation.
  • Persisting the fix in /etc/network/interfaces is better than relying on manual reapplication after reboot.

docs.title: Follow-Up Operational Notes After Stability Improved
Summary
docs.text: After roughly a day of runtime, the node appeared stable with most containers running. The conversation then shifted into follow-up operational guidance for other NUC nodes, VM disk option tuning, backup option clarification, and Proxmox update workflow.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Proxmox host: pve1
  • Other nodes: Intel NUC8i7BEH
  • VM: debian-docker
  • Proxmox storage and VM disk options discussed:
  • IOThreads
  • discard
  • cloud-init disk
  • per-disk backup checkbox

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: With immediate instability reduced, the next goal was to standardize the workaround and document safe operational behavior.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Node appeared stable after the NIC workaround.
  • A Proxmox warning occurred when enabling IOThread:
  • WARN: iothread is only valid with virtio disk or virtio-scsi-single controller, ignoring
  • Clarification was needed on:
  • whether to apply the NIC fix to other NUCs
  • whether IOThreads/discard were appropriate
  • where backup storage is actually consumed
  • how to update Proxmox cleanly

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Determined that the NIC workaround should likely be repeated on other NUC8i7BEH nodes using the same onboard NIC and driver.
  2. Reviewed whether iothread and discard were appropriate for the VM disk.
  3. Explained that the per-disk Proxmox “Backup” checkbox only controls inclusion in backups and does not allocate separate disk storage on its own.
  4. Interpreted the IOThread warning as a controller/disk compatibility issue.
  5. Documented concise Proxmox update commands and Proxmox wrapper behavior.

Important commands used or discussed:

pveversion

Purpose: check current Proxmox version before updating.

apt update
apt full-upgrade -y
reboot

Purpose: standard Proxmox update flow.

pveupdate
pveupgrade
reboot

Purpose: wrapper-based Proxmox update flow.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The Intel NUC8i7BEH platform likely shares the same e1000e risk on other nodes, so the workaround should be standardized there as well.
  • iothread is only valid for:
  • virtio disks
  • disks on a virtio-scsi-single controller
  • Enabling iothread on unsupported disks such as cloud-init or unsupported controller types produces a warning and is ignored.
  • discard=on is generally reasonable for thin-provisioned or Ceph-backed virtual disks when trim/unmap is supported.
  • The Proxmox disk “Backup” option only controls whether a disk is included in a VM backup job.
  • pveupdate and pveupgrade are Proxmox convenience wrappers around the normal apt flow.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: Current status: - pve1 appeared stable after the NIC offload workaround. - Guidance was captured for: - repeating the NIC fix on similar NUC nodes - using IOThread only on supported disk/controller combinations - enabling discard where appropriate - understanding disk-backup inclusion behavior - updating Proxmox using either standard apt or Proxmox wrappers

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Roughly one day of improved stability was observed with most containers running.
  • No new failure evidence was presented during this follow-up checkpoint.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Apply the NIC workaround across other NUC8i7BEH nodes.
  • Review BIOS versions and cooling health on all NUC nodes.
  • Verify VM controller types before enabling IOThreads.
  • Continue observing pve1 before fully closing the incident.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Once a platform-specific fault pattern is confirmed, standardizing the workaround across identical nodes is usually worthwhile.
  • Not all Proxmox disk options apply to all controller types.
  • Concise operational notes are useful once an incident moves from active troubleshooting to maintenance.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: journalctl --list-boots

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Lists known boot sessions from the systemd journal, each with a relative boot index such as 0, -1, or -2.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • none in this invocation

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To identify which earlier boot corresponded to a failure window before inspecting previous-boot logs.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A list of boots with IDs and time ranges.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: prior boots are available for review.
  • Failure: journald may not be persistent or older logs may be unavailable.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: journalctl -k -b -1

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows kernel messages from the previous boot.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -k: kernel messages only
  • -b -1: previous boot

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To inspect host-side kernel and driver behavior leading up to the last crash or reboot.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The previous boot’s kernel log.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: prior-boot kernel history is available.
  • Failure: logs from the prior boot are missing.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: journalctl -k -b -1 -p warning..alert

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Filters the previous boot’s kernel log to warnings and more severe messages.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • -p warning..alert: severity range from warning through alert

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To reduce noise and focus only on significant prior-boot kernel warnings and errors.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A smaller, higher-signal set of kernel messages.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: serious kernel messages are easier to inspect.
  • No output: there may have been no warning-level kernel events captured.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: journalctl -k -b -1 | egrep -i 'panic|BUG:|Oops|Call Trace|hardware error|MCE|watchdog|soft lockup|hard lockup|NMI|reset|blocked for more than'

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Searches the previous boot’s kernel log for common panic, fault, and hardware-error signatures.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• egrep -i: extended regex, case-insensitive
  • Search terms include common kernel crash indicators

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To quickly identify whether the failure resembled a classic panic, watchdog event, or MCE-style hardware fault.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Any matching fault signatures.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Matches found: there may be direct crash or hardware clues.
  • No matches: the failure may be outside classic kernel panic patterns.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: free -h

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays memory usage in human-readable units.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • -h: human-readable output

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To check whether host RAM exhaustion was contributing to instability.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A summary showing total, used, free, shared, cache, and available memory.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• High available memory: RAM starvation is less likely.
  • Very low available memory plus swap activity: memory pressure is more likely.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: swapon --show

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Lists configured swap devices and current swap usage.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • --show: tabular display of active swap devices

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To verify whether the host had started swapping.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A list of swap devices and used size.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• 0B used: no current swap pressure.
  • Nonzero usage: swap pressure exists or existed.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: vmstat 1 5

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Prints virtual memory, process, IO, swap, and CPU statistics every second for five samples.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• 1: sample interval in seconds
  • 5: number of samples

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To check whether the host was swapping, blocked on IO, or under visible CPU pressure.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Five rows of live system telemetry.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• si/so above zero: active swap activity.
  • High wa: IO wait / storage bottleneck.
  • High run queue or low idle: CPU pressure.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo journalctl -kf

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Follows live kernel messages continuously.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -k: kernel messages only
  • -f: follow new entries as they arrive

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To observe live guest or host kernel behavior during runtime and while reproducing the issue.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: New kernel messages appear as they are logged.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• New fault messages during a problem window can reveal the root cause.
  • Quiet output may simply mean the kernel is not logging anything unusual.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: apt-get install -y lm-sensors

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Installs Linux hardware sensor utilities and dependencies.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • -y: automatically answer yes to prompts

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To gather thermal telemetry from the NUC host.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The lm-sensors package and dependencies install successfully.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: thermal readings can be collected.
  • Failure: package or repository issue must be resolved first.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sensors-detect

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Probes the system for supported hardware monitoring chips and recommended drivers.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • interactive prompts during hardware probing

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To identify which driver modules were needed for temperature reporting.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A detection summary showing supported sensors and recommended modules.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: usable drivers are identified.
  • Failure: the platform may expose only limited monitoring.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low to moderate. Some bus probing is more intrusive than simply reading existing sensors.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Run only sensors first if sensor modules are already loaded, but sensors-detect is standard when telemetry is missing.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sensors

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays current temperature and sensor readings.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • none in this invocation

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To inspect CPU, chipset, and NVMe temperatures while evaluating thermal behavior.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Temperature readings per detected device.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• High temperatures near critical thresholds indicate thermal stress.
  • Normal temperatures during failure windows suggest another fault domain exists.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: watch -n2 sensors

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Repeats the sensors command every two seconds.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • -n2: update every two seconds

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To catch peak temperatures under sustained load rather than relying on a single snapshot.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A continuously refreshed thermal display.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• CPU temperatures near 99–100°C indicate thermal throttle territory on this NUC.
  • Later normal readings during a crash showed thermal issues were not the only problem.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: journalctl -k -b -1 | tail -n 120

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows the last 120 lines of the previous boot’s kernel log.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • tail -n 120: limit to the end of the log where the failure likely occurred

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To inspect the final kernel events before the reboot.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The previous boot’s last kernel messages.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• This command exposed the repeated e1000e NIC hang messages.
  • If the log ends abruptly with no clue, hard power loss or deeper lockup remains possible.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: journalctl -b -1 -p err..alert | tail -n 120

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows the last 120 error-level and higher messages from the previous boot.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -b -1: previous boot
  • -p err..alert: error through alert severity
  • tail -n 120: limit output to the end of the failure window

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To isolate high-severity service and kernel errors from the failing boot.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A compact list of severe prior-boot messages.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text: • This helped confirm the NIC hang as the main severe event before reboot.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: journalctl -b -1 | egrep -i 'panic|BUG:|Call Trace|watchdog|soft lockup|hard lockup|mce|hardware error|fatal|reset|reboot' | tail -n 80

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Searches the previous boot’s full journal for panic, watchdog, and hardware-fault signatures.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• egrep -i: case-insensitive regex search
  • tail -n 80: focus on the end of the result set

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To distinguish a NIC or network failure from a classic kernel panic or hardware fault.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Any matching critical fault lines.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Few or no relevant matches support a non-panic failure path.
  • Strong matches would shift attention back to kernel or hardware-fault analysis.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: last -x | head

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows recent login, reboot, shutdown, and runlevel events.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -x: include system events such as reboot and runlevel changes
  • head: limit to the most recent entries

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To confirm reboot timing and how long the failed boot lasted.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Recent reboot and runlevel history.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Frequent reboot entries confirm repeated outages.
  • Helps align journal timestamps with observed downtime.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: apt-get install -y ethtool

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Installs the NIC inspection and tuning utility.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • -y: automatically answer yes

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To disable problematic offload features on the Intel onboard NIC.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: ethtool installs successfully.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: NIC features can be queried and changed.
  • Failure: NIC mitigation steps cannot be applied yet.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ethtool -K eno1 tso off gso off gro off

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Disables transmit segmentation offload, generic segmentation offload, and generic receive offload on the physical NIC.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -K: change offload settings
  • tso off: disable TCP segmentation offload
  • gso off: disable generic segmentation offload
  • gro off: disable generic receive offload

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To avoid the offload paths associated with the observed e1000e hardware hangs on the Intel NUC NIC.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The command succeeds and the requested offloads are disabled.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: the NIC is less likely to hit the buggy offload path.
  • Failure: the feature is unsupported or the change was rejected.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low. Performance may decrease slightly because packet processing shifts more into software.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Disabling only one feature at a time is sometimes used for narrower testing, but disabling all three was the chosen mitigation here.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ethtool -K vmbr0 tso off gso off gro off

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Requests the same offload-related changes on the Linux bridge device.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • same offload flags as above

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To align bridge-layer behavior with the NIC workaround where supported.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Some bridge features may change; others may remain fixed or partially supported.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Mixed bridge output is normal.
  • The critical mitigation remains the change on the physical NIC.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: The physical NIC change alone is the essential step.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ethtool -k eno1 | egrep 'tso|gso|gro'

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays the relevant offload settings for the physical NIC.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -k: show NIC feature state
  • egrep 'tso|gso|gro': filter only relevant offload entries

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To verify that the workaround was actually in effect on eno1.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Relevant offloads show off or off [fixed].

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text: • Correct output confirms the physical NIC mitigation is active.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ethtool -k vmbr0 | egrep 'tso|gso|gro'

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays the relevant offload-related settings for the bridge.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -k: show feature state
  • egrep 'tso|gso|gro': filter relevant entries

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To inspect bridge-level behavior after applying the workaround.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A mix of bridge-specific feature states.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text: • Use this as supplemental verification only; eno1 is the important interface.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: nano /etc/network/interfaces

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Opens the Proxmox host network configuration file for editing.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • none

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To persist the ethtool workaround with post-up lines.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The network configuration file opens in the editor.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: the workaround can survive reboot.
  • Failure: another editor or permission check is needed.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Incorrect edits can break management networking on the host.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: The Proxmox GUI is safer for common network changes, but direct file edits are often necessary for custom post-up directives.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ifreload -a

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Reloads all interface definitions using ifupdown2.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • -a: reload all interfaces

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To apply the persistent NIC workaround without rebooting the node.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Interfaces reload successfully and post-up hooks run.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: the persistent change is live immediately.
  • Failure: there may be a syntax issue in /etc/network/interfaces.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Reloading networking on a remote Proxmox host can interrupt management access if the config is wrong.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Reboot during a maintenance window instead of live reloading.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: pveversion

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows installed Proxmox VE version information.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • none

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To check the current update baseline before upgrading the node.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Proxmox version output.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text: • Success: confirms current installed version state.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: apt update

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Refreshes package metadata from configured Debian and Proxmox repositories.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • none

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: As part of the standard Proxmox node update workflow.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Updated package lists.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: host is ready for package upgrade.
  • Failure: repository, network, or configuration problem exists.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: pveupdate is the Proxmox wrapper for a similar step.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: apt full-upgrade -y

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Performs a full package upgrade, allowing dependency changes and replacements.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• full-upgrade: upgrade with dependency changes
  • -y: automatically confirm prompts

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To fully update the Proxmox node, including kernel and core packages.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The node upgrades all eligible packages.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: node is updated and ready for reboot.
  • Failure: package dependency or repository issues must be addressed.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. This can update critical Proxmox, kernel, and storage components.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Run on one node at a time during a maintenance window.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: reboot

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Restarts the node.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • none

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To load updated kernels or apply changes that require reboot.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The node restarts and returns to service.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: host comes back online with the updated runtime.
  • Failure: console investigation may be required.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: High in a clustered environment if workloads are not planned around the reboot.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Migrate or stop critical workloads first.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: pveupdate

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Runs the Proxmox convenience wrapper for refreshing package lists.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • none

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To describe the concise Proxmox-native update flow.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Repository metadata refreshes.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: package lists are current.
  • Failure: same classes of issues as apt update.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: apt update is the standard Debian equivalent.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: pveupgrade

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Runs the Proxmox convenience wrapper for a full node upgrade.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • none in this invocation

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To describe the concise Proxmox-native update flow.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Available Proxmox and Debian package upgrades are applied.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: host is updated.
  • Failure: dependency or repository issues need review.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. This can affect critical virtualization and storage components.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: apt full-upgrade is the standard Debian equivalent.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: Likely command used: top

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays live process, CPU, memory, and load information.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• interactive usage
  • in a VM, pressing 1 shows per-vCPU detail

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To inspect runtime CPU pressure, IO wait, and possible VM steal time.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: An interactive process and CPU summary.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• High wa: storage bottleneck.
  • High st inside a VM: host scheduling contention.
  • High load with poor responsiveness: potential lockup or backend dependency issue.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: htop if installed, but top is standard and usually available.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: Likely command used: iostat -x 1

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays extended disk IO statistics every second.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -x: extended device statistics
  • 1: one-second interval

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: To determine whether storage latency or device saturation was contributing to freezes.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Rolling per-device IO stats including utilization and wait times.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• High %util and await indicate a storage bottleneck.
  • Low utilization suggests storage is not the main limiter.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: Likely command used: stress-ng --cpu 8 --vm 2 --vm-bytes 75% --io 4 --timeout 30m

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Generates synthetic CPU, memory, and IO load on the host.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• --cpu 8: stress 8 CPU workers
  • --vm 2: memory stress workers
  • --vm-bytes 75%: target 75% memory usage
  • --io 4: IO stress workers
  • --timeout 30m: run for 30 minutes

docs.title: Why it was discussed at that moment
docs.text: To separate generic hardware instability from workload-specific failures.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Thirty minutes of sustained synthetic load.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Stable run: hardware may be okay and workload pattern may matter more.
  • Crash/hang: deeper hardware, firmware, or kernel issues become more likely.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate to high. Can push the node into failure and should be run only in a maintenance window.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Run a shorter-duration test first.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: Likely command used: memtest86+ / MemTest86 boot run

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Tests system RAM outside the normal operating system.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • boot-time utility rather than a shell command in this session

docs.title: Why it was discussed at that moment
docs.text: To rule out faulty RAM after repeated host instability.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Multiple clean passes with zero errors.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Zero errors: RAM is less likely to be the issue.
  • Any errors: memory or memory path faults are strongly suspected.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low runtime risk, but requires host downtime.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None equivalent from inside the running OS.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: Likely command used: qm / Proxmox GUI disk option changes for iothread and discard

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Applies VM disk options such as IOThreads and discard/TRIM handling.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• iothread: valid on supported virtio/virtio-scsi-single disk paths
  • discard: enables trim/unmap propagation when supported

docs.title: Why it was discussed at that moment
docs.text: To improve VM disk behavior and explain why Proxmox ignored IOThread on an unsupported disk/controller combination.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Supported disks accept the setting; unsupported ones emit a warning and ignore it.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Warning about unsupported controller or disk type means IOThread is ignored safely.
  • Correct use requires a virtio disk or virtio-scsi-single controller.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low to moderate depending on whether controller changes require downtime.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Verify disk bus and SCSI controller type before enabling IOThread.

docs.title: Networking
docs.text: Documents are ordered by logical event flow.

docs.title: Documents
docs.text:
• att-fiber-opnsense-outage-runbook.md
  • opnsense-web-gui-documentation-and-runbook.md
  • opnsense-lan-ip-dhcp-static-device-runbook.md
  • opnsense-opt2-lan-runbook.md
  • opnsense-lan-instability-storm-control-runbook.md
  • wireguard-ec2-proxmox-lxc-homelab-remote-access.md

docs.title: AT&T Fiber Outage (ONT / OPNsense WAN Loss)
Summary
docs.text: Troubleshooting a sudden internet outage in a homelab environment using OPNsense with AT&T fiber. The issue was isolated to the upstream fiber connection (ONT) and resolved via power cycling.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Router/Firewall: OPNsense
  • ISP: AT&T Fiber
  • Gateway: BGW320
  • ONT (Optical Network Terminal): External fiber termination device
  • LAN: Homelab network (Proxmox cluster, Docker workloads, etc.)
  • Topology:
  • Fiber → ONT → AT&T Gateway (BGW320 or passthrough) → OPNsense → LAN

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Complete loss of internet connectivity across the homelab network.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• No internet access from LAN devices
  • Likely failed external connectivity (e.g., ping to public IPs)
  • ONT displayed a red light prior to recovery
  • AT&T gateway not in normal solid white state during issue

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Observed physical device status (ONT and AT&T gateway LEDs)
  2. Performed a power cycle of the ONT
  3. Allowed ONT to fully reinitialize and re-establish fiber link
  4. Verified gateway returned to normal (solid white) state

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Red light on ONT indicated Loss of Signal (LOS) or upstream fiber issue
  • Issue was not caused by:
  • OPNsense configuration
  • Internal networking (LAN, VLANs, firewall rules)
  • Likely causes:
  • Temporary ISP-side disruption
  • Optical signal drop or PON re-sync requirement
  • AT&T fiber can exhibit MAC/session stickiness, requiring ONT reset to renegotiate connection

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Power cycling the ONT restored fiber signal and connectivity
  • Network returned to normal operation without additional configuration changes

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• AT&T gateway returned to solid white (healthy state)
  • Internet connectivity restored across LAN devices
  • Implicit validation: upstream routing, DNS, and WAN connectivity functional

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Place ONT and gateway on a UPS to prevent transient outages
  • Document WAN interface name on OPNsense for faster troubleshooting
  • Capture logs during future outages:
  • /var/log/system.log
  • Consider monitoring:
  • WAN gateway status (dpinger)
  • Interface flaps
  • (Optional) Review AT&T passthrough / bypass configuration for stability

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Physical layer issues can mimic higher-layer failures (DNS, routing, firewall)
  • Always check ONT status lights first in fiber setups
  • Power cycling ONT is often required due to:
  • PON re-authentication
  • ISP MAC/session binding behavior
  • LED meanings differ:
  • BGW320: white = healthy
  • ONT: green = healthy, red = signal failure
  • Avoid unnecessary troubleshooting of OPNsense until WAN link is confirmed up

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ping 1.1.1.1

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Tests basic IP connectivity to an external host (Cloudflare DNS).

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To determine whether the issue was: - Layer 3 connectivity (WAN down), or - DNS-related

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text:
• Success → WAN is up, issue likely DNS
  • Failure → WAN or upstream connectivity issue

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ifconfig -a

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays all network interfaces and their status.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Used to identify the WAN interface and check: - Link state - Assigned IP address

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text:
• WAN interface should show:
  • status: active
  • Valid public IP

docs.title: Command
docs.text: netstat -rn4 | grep ^default

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays the IPv4 routing table default gateway.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Confirms whether a valid default route exists for outbound traffic.

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text: • Valid default gateway via WAN interface

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
/sbin/dhclient -r <wanif>
/sbin/dhclient <wanif>

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Releases and renews DHCP lease on the WAN interface.

docs.title: Why it was suggested
docs.text: To recover WAN connectivity without rebooting hardware.

docs.title: Important Notes
docs.text:
• <wanif> = actual WAN interface (e.g., igb0, ix0)
  • Safe to run

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text:
• New IP lease assigned
  • WAN connectivity restored

docs.title: Command
docs.text: clog /var/log/system.log | tail -50

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays recent system logs on OPNsense.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: To check: - DHCP failures - Gateway status - Interface errors

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text:
• Successful DHCP lease messages
  • No persistent WAN errors

docs.title: Command
docs.text: configctl interface reconfigure wan

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Reinitializes the WAN interface in OPNsense.

docs.title: Why it was suggested
docs.text: To recover from gateway or dpinger issues without rebooting.

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text:
• WAN interface resets
  • Gateway status restored

docs.title: Command
docs.text: configctl unbound restart

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Restarts the Unbound DNS resolver.

docs.title: Why it was suggested
docs.text: Used when: - IP connectivity works - DNS resolution fails

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text: • DNS resolution restored

docs.title: Command
docs.text: drill cloudflare.com @1.1.1.1

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Performs a DNS lookup using a specific DNS server.

docs.title: Why it was suggested
docs.text: To isolate DNS issues: - Tests external DNS directly

docs.title: Expected Result
docs.text:
• Valid DNS response → DNS working
  • Failure → DNS or upstream issue

docs.title: OPNsense LAN Instability, AP/OPT Segmentation Review, and TL-SG108E Storm-Control Stabilization
Summary
docs.text:
This session focused on troubleshooting intermittent LAN and internet connectivity problems in a homelab network built around OPNsense, multiple TP-Link TL-SG108E smart switches, and TP-Link Archer access points. The original investigation began around a question of whether an OPT interface and a second downstream router were causing client internet failures. During troubleshooting, it was clarified that one downstream TP-Link device was already operating in access point mode rather than router mode, shifting attention back to the main LAN path.

The issue was ultimately narrowed to instability on the flat LAN segment rather than OPT1. The network was simplified conceptually as a flat switched network, and switch storm-control settings were applied uniformly. A flat storm-control threshold of 8000 Kbps on all switches appeared to stabilize the environment.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Router/firewall: OPNsense running on a CWWK 12th-gen Intel mini PC with Intel i3-N305
  • Primary LAN switching:
  • 4 × TP-Link TL-SG108E Easy Smart switches
  • Wireless infrastructure:
  • TP-Link Archer AX80 in AP mode on LAN
  • TP-Link Archer AX72 Pro in AP mode on OPT1
  • Wired clients mentioned:
  • Proxmox nodes
  • Android TV box
  • OPNsense interfaces:
  • LAN
  • OPT1
  • Logical network design during this session:
  • Flat LAN on the main switched path
  • Separate OPT1 segment feeding the Archer AX72 Pro in AP mode
  • Services discussed:
  • OPNsense DHCP
  • OPNsense Unbound DNS
  • Firewall rules and outbound NAT
  • Switch storm control
  • Loop prevention
  • VLANs:
  • Discussed conceptually only
  • Not implemented during this session

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Devices on the main LAN were slow to connect, sometimes failed to get internet access after connecting, and wired devices downstream of the smart switches were also losing connectivity. There was initial uncertainty about whether the issue was related to OPT1 segmentation, downstream TP-Link operating mode, DHCP behavior, or broader layer-2 instability.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Devices were slow to connect to the network
  • Once connected, devices often had no internet access
  • Wired devices connected through the smart-switch chain also lost connectivity
  • OPNsense logs showed some default deny and state-violation entries
  • There was uncertainty about whether a second downstream TP-Link was acting as a router or only as an AP
  • Connectivity problems affected both wireless and wired clients on the main LAN path

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Reviewed the original physical topology:
  2. OPNsense LAN → TL-SG108E core switch → additional TL-SG108E switches → Proxmox nodes, Android TV box, and Archer AX80 in AP mode

  3. OPNsense OPT1 → Archer AX72 Pro in AP mode

  4. Considered whether the downstream TP-Link on the secondary path should operate as:

  5. a router behind an OPNsense OPT interface, or

  6. a pure AP bridged into the OPNsense segment

  7. Clarified that the Archer on the secondary segment was already in AP mode, not router mode.

  8. Determined that OPT1 appeared to be functioning correctly and shifted focus to the main LAN path.

  9. Reviewed likely causes on LAN:

  10. layer-2 loops
  11. broadcast or multicast storms
  12. rogue DHCP
  13. AP uplink misconfiguration

  14. switch-chain instability

  15. Verified or discussed AP best practices:

  16. AP mode enabled
  17. DHCP disabled on APs
  18. uplink should use LAN port rather than WAN in AP mode

  19. management IPs should be static or reserved

  20. Discussed assigning static IP addresses to the Archer APs for management.

  21. Reviewed TL-SG108E management considerations:

  22. password-reset path if login access was lost

  23. later confirmed switch login was still using the default credentials

  24. Reviewed TL-SG108E VLAN features conceptually:

  25. MTU VLAN
  26. Port-Based VLAN
  27. 802.1Q VLAN
  28. PVID behavior

  29. Confirmed VLANs were not needed at this time

  30. Confirmed that loop prevention was already enabled by default on the switches.

  31. Applied storm-control settings uniformly across the switches at a flat threshold of 8000 Kbps to simplify the configuration.

  32. Observed that the storm-control change appeared to resolve the immediate LAN instability.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The problem path was the main LAN, not OPT1.
  • The secondary TP-Link device was already in AP mode, so the issue was not caused by an intended router-behind-OPT design.
  • Because the AP was bridged, clients behind it should have been using OPNsense-provided DHCP, gateway, and DNS rather than a separate routed path.
  • The symptoms matched a likely layer-2 issue more closely than a routing issue:
  • intermittent connectivity
  • slow association
  • wired and wireless impact
  • apparent stabilization after storm-control changes
  • Loop prevention was already enabled, but that alone was not sufficient to fully suppress the instability.
  • Applying storm control at 8000 Kbps on all switches appeared to mitigate the problem.
  • VLAN support on the TL-SG108E and OPNsense was discussed for future use, but VLANs were not part of the implemented fix.

docs.title: Facts
docs.text:
• OPT1 appeared stable during this session.
  • Archer AX72 Pro was confirmed to be in AP mode.
  • Archer AX80 was also being used in AP mode on LAN.
  • There were actually 4 TL-SG108E switches in the environment, not 3.
  • Loop prevention was enabled by default.
  • Storm control set to 8000 Kbps across the switches appeared to solve the issue for the time being.

docs.title: Assumptions / Working Theories
docs.text:
• The root cause was likely excessive broadcast, multicast, unknown unicast, or other layer-2 flood behavior on the flat LAN.
  • A loop, bursty discovery traffic, or MAC-table churn may have contributed even if loop prevention was enabled.
  • Rogue DHCP remained a theoretical possibility earlier in the investigation, but no direct evidence in this conversation confirmed it as the final cause.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
The practical workaround and current working fix was to keep the network flat and enable switch storm control uniformly at 8000 Kbps across the TL-SG108E switches. This simplified the configuration and appears to have stabilized LAN behavior.

No VLAN design was deployed during this work session. OPT1 remained separate from LAN, but the main issue was treated as a LAN switching problem rather than a firewall or routed-interface problem.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Success was validated informally by observed behavior after the switch change: - LAN connectivity appeared stable - Internet access returned for affected devices - The user reported that storm control seemed to have solved the issue - A flat 8000 Kbps threshold was retained because it worked and was easy to manage

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Change the default admin password on all TL-SG108E switches
  • Assign static management IPs to all TL-SG108E switches
  • Assign static management IPs or DHCP reservations to both Archer APs
  • Document exact switch port mappings:
  • core switch uplink to OPNsense
  • inter-switch uplinks
  • Proxmox-node ports
  • AP ports
  • Android TV box port
  • Back up switch configurations after confirming stability
  • Continue monitoring for:
  • renewed packet loss
  • discovery failures
  • intermittent wired drops
  • multicast-heavy application issues
  • Consider raising multicast or unknown-unicast storm thresholds on switch uplinks later if application-specific issues appear
  • Verify that only OPNsense is providing DHCP on the LAN segment
  • Disable EEE/Green Ethernet on uplinks, AP ports, and server ports if not already done
  • Keep VLAN planning deferred until the flat network remains stable over time

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Do not assume a downstream TP-Link is routing; confirm whether it is in AP mode or router mode before designing around NAT or firewall behavior.
  • If both wired and wireless clients are unstable on the same flat LAN, investigate layer-2 conditions before focusing on routing.
  • Loop prevention alone may not be enough to stabilize a noisy switched environment.
  • A simple, flat storm-control policy can be a useful first stabilization step in a small homelab.
  • Keep management IPs for APs and switches fixed and documented.
  • Avoid adding VLAN complexity until the physical topology and baseline switching behavior are stable.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ipconfig

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays IP configuration on Windows clients.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Used or implied for checking whether a client received the correct IP address, default gateway, and DNS server.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A client on the LAN should receive: - an IP in the LAN subnet - default gateway equal to the OPNsense LAN IP - DNS pointing to OPNsense or the intended DNS path

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Success: client is likely getting correct DHCP information
  • Failure: wrong gateway or DNS may indicate rogue DHCP, AP/router misconfiguration, or subnet mismatch

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ifconfig

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays interface configuration on Unix-like systems.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Implied as the Linux/macOS equivalent of ipconfig for confirming client IP settings.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The client interface should show the correct subnet, address, and routing context.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Correct interface details support DHCP and addressing health
  • Incorrect subnet or no address points toward DHCP or physical connectivity issues

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ping 8.8.8.8

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Tests raw IP connectivity without depending on DNS.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Used conceptually to distinguish routing/internet problems from DNS problems.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Replies from 8.8.8.8 if the client has working connectivity to the internet.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Success: routing and outbound connectivity are likely working
  • Failure: internet routing, firewall, NAT, or upstream path may be broken

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk. A more policy-neutral test target in some environments may be the ISP gateway or another known reachable IP.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ping example.com

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Tests both DNS resolution and connectivity.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Used conceptually to determine whether hostname resolution was working after a raw IP ping test.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The hostname should resolve and the destination should reply.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• If ping 8.8.8.8 works but this fails, DNS is the likely problem
  • If both fail, the issue is probably broader than DNS

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: nslookup example.com

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Queries DNS directly for a hostname.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Implied for validating whether OPNsense Unbound DNS was reachable and resolving names correctly.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The command should return a valid DNS response from the intended resolver.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Success: DNS service path is functioning
  • Failure: resolver access, Unbound interface binding, access lists, or upstream resolution may be broken

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: traceroute 8.8.8.8

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows the path packets take toward a destination on Unix-like systems.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Implied for checking whether traffic was traversing the expected local gateway path.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The first hop should be the local router for that segment, followed by upstream path entries.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Correct first hop confirms the expected default gateway
  • Unexpected first hops can reveal hidden NAT, wrong gateway assignment, or routing asymmetry

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk.

docs.title: Safer / platform note
docs.text:
On Windows, the equivalent is:

tracert 8.8.8.8

docs.title: Command
docs.text: tracert 8.8.8.8

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Windows equivalent of traceroute.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Suggested conceptually to verify that clients were using the correct gateway and path.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The first hop should be the local router for the client subnet.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Correct path supports proper DHCP and routing
  • Incorrect path suggests gateway or topology problems

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk.

docs.title: Likely command used
docs.text: arp -a

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays the ARP cache on many client systems.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: A likely troubleshooting step for checking IP-to-MAC relationships and detecting duplicate IP behavior or unexpected gateways.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The gateway IP should map to the expected OPNsense MAC address.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Expected mapping supports correct layer-2 forwarding
  • Flapping or unexpected MACs can suggest duplicate addressing or rogue infrastructure

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk.

docs.title: OPNsense action
docs.text: Diagnostics → States → Reset States

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Clears the firewall state table in OPNsense.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Suggested after topology changes or interface-path changes to remove stale states that can produce state-violation logs or inconsistent connectivity.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Existing connections are briefly interrupted, then rebuild using current topology and policy.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Improvement afterward suggests stale states contributed to the issue
  • No improvement suggests the root cause is elsewhere

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Moderate operational impact. This is disruptive to active sessions and should be used carefully during production traffic.

docs.title: OPNsense action
docs.text: Services → DHCPv4 → Leases

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays active DHCP leases on an interface.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Used to verify that clients were receiving addresses from OPNsense rather than from a rogue DHCP server.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Affected clients should appear with addresses in the correct subnet.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Expected leases support correct DHCP behavior
  • Missing or inconsistent leases may indicate DHCP conflict or wrong segment placement

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk. Read-only diagnostic view.

docs.title: OPNsense action
docs.text: Services → Unbound DNS → General

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Controls Unbound DNS listener interfaces and resolver behavior.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Discussed in detail because DNS issues can look like internet failures even when routing works.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Unbound should listen on the intended internal interfaces and permit the intended client subnets.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Correct configuration allows reliable name resolution
  • Misconfiguration can cause clients to appear “online but without internet”

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk when reviewing. Changing settings may affect DNS for multiple subnets.

docs.title: OPNsense action
docs.text: Firewall → Rules → LAN

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Shows and manages firewall rules for the LAN interface.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: A standard check when determining whether the problem is routing/firewall-related or truly layer-2-related.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A permissive LAN rule set should allow normal outbound traffic during basic troubleshooting.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• If rules are correct and the issue persists, the cause is more likely switching, DHCP, or DNS
  • Misordered or missing rules can block traffic in ways that mimic network instability

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Moderate risk if modified. Firewall changes affect reachability immediately.

docs.title: OPNsense action
docs.text: Firewall → NAT → Outbound

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Controls outbound NAT policy.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Discussed when evaluating whether OPT-style routed networks were missing internet due to NAT configuration.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Internal subnets requiring internet access should be translated correctly on WAN.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Correct NAT allows outbound internet access
  • Missing NAT on a routed subnet breaks internet access even when local connectivity works

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Moderate risk if changed. Incorrect outbound NAT can disrupt all egress traffic.

docs.title: OPNsense action
docs.text: Interfaces → Assignments / Interfaces → OPT1

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Assigns and configures routed interfaces such as OPT1.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Used conceptually when determining whether a second router or AP on OPT1 should be routed or bridged.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: OPT1 should have the intended subnet and service scope if used as a separate network.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• A healthy OPT1 with working clients suggests the main issue is elsewhere
  • Misconfiguration would isolate clients on that segment

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Moderate risk if changed. Interface changes can interrupt connected devices.

docs.title: Switch action
docs.text: QoS → Storm Control

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Applies per-port thresholds to suppress excessive broadcast, multicast, or unknown-unicast traffic on the TL-SG108E switches.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: This became the key stabilization step in the session.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Flood behavior should be limited enough to prevent LAN instability while still allowing normal traffic.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Improvement after enabling or adjusting storm control suggests a layer-2 flood condition was contributing
  • Overly aggressive thresholds may suppress legitimate traffic

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Moderate operational risk. Improper thresholds can interfere with legitimate traffic such as discovery or multicast-heavy applications.

docs.title: Switch action
docs.text: Loop Prevention / Loopback Detection

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Attempts to detect and suppress switching loops on the TL-SG108E platform.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Reviewed because a loop or loop-like condition was one of the primary suspected causes of the LAN instability.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The switch should detect and mitigate loop conditions automatically.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• If enabled but instability persists, additional controls such as storm control may still be required
  • A disabled state would increase exposure to accidental loops

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low risk to enable. Important baseline protection in daisy-chained homelab switch topologies.

docs.title: Switch action
docs.text: Advanced → Network → LAN

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Used on the Archer APs to set or review the management IP address.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Static IP assignment for AP management was part of the cleanup and hardening discussion.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Each AP receives a stable management address in the correct subnet and outside the DHCP pool.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Correct IPs make AP administration predictable
  • Wrong subnet or overlapping DHCP use can cause management-plane confusion

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Low to moderate risk. Changing the management IP can briefly disconnect the web session until reconnecting to the new address.

docs.title: Switch / AP action
docs.text: Factory reset via recessed reset button

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Restores the switch or AP to factory defaults.

docs.title: Why it was relevant
docs.text: Discussed as a recovery method for TL-SG108E login access before it was discovered that the switches were still using the default credentials.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Default management access and default credentials are restored.

docs.title: What success or failure indicates
docs.text:
• Successful reset restores admin access
  • It also removes custom configuration, requiring reconfiguration afterward

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: High operational risk. Use only when necessary and after recording current settings if possible.

docs.title: OPNsense LAN IP Change & DHCP / Static Device Issues
Summary
docs.text: Changed the LAN subnet on an OPNsense firewall and encountered issues where multiple devices, including Proxmox and OMV hosts, were not appearing on the network. Troubleshooting focused on DHCP configuration, static IP conflicts, and subnet mismatches.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Firewall: OPNsense
  • Hypervisor: Proxmox
  • Storage: OMV / OpenMediaVault NAS
  • Network:
  • Old subnet: 192.168.1.0/24
  • New subnet: 192.168.16.0/24
  • DHCP: OPNsense DHCPv4 server
  • Devices:
  • Proxmox host(s)
  • OMV NAS
  • Other LAN clients using a mix of DHCP and static IPs

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: After changing the LAN IP/subnet in OPNsense, several devices, notably Proxmox and OMV, were no longer visible or reachable on the LAN.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Devices did not appear in OPNsense DHCP leases.
  • Some devices were missing from LAN visibility checks.
  • Proxmox and OMV web interfaces were not reachable.
  • Some DHCP clients worked while static-IP devices appeared to disappear.
  • Static devices were likely still using the old LAN subnet.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Changed the OPNsense LAN IP using the console menu:
  2. 2) Set interface IP address
  3. Reviewed the IPv6 console prompt:
  4. Configure IPv6 address LAN interface via WAN tracking?
  5. Determined that IPv6 WAN tracking should be skipped/disabled unless the ISP provides IPv6 Prefix Delegation.
  6. Configured DHCPv4 for the LAN interface.
  7. Discussed DHCP range planning:
  8. Example DHCP pool: 192.168.16.21 through 192.168.16.254
  9. Reserved static range: 192.168.16.2 through 192.168.16.20
  10. Identified that several affected devices used static IPs.
  11. Determined that static devices configured for the old subnet would not request DHCP leases and would not appear in the DHCP table.
  12. Recommended updating static devices to the new subnet or converting them to DHCP reservations.
  13. Discussed using a temporary LAN alias for the old subnet as a migration workaround if needed.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Static IP devices do not request DHCP leases, so they will not appear in OPNsense DHCP lease tables.
  • Devices configured with old-subnet addresses such as 192.168.1.x cannot directly communicate with a new LAN subnet such as 192.168.16.x/24.
  • A DHCP range of .21-.254 leaves .2-.20 available for manually assigned static infrastructure addresses.
  • Gateway and DNS settings must also be updated on static devices.
  • For a LAN using 192.168.16.1/24, static devices should generally use:
  • IP address: 192.168.16.2 through 192.168.16.20, if reserved for static use
  • Gateway: 192.168.16.1
  • DNS: 192.168.16.1 or another intended DNS resolver

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
The likely root cause was that Proxmox, OMV, and other manually configured devices still had static IP settings from the old LAN subnet.

The corrective action is to update each static device to the new subnet. Example:

  • OPNsense LAN IP: 192.168.16.1/24
  • Proxmox static IP: 192.168.16.2
  • OMV static IP: 192.168.16.3
  • Gateway: 192.168.16.1
  • DNS: 192.168.16.1 or another configured DNS resolver

The DHCP range can safely remain:

192.168.16.21 - 192.168.16.254

This leaves:

192.168.16.2 - 192.168.16.20

for manually configured infrastructure devices.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Validation should include:

  1. Confirm OPNsense LAN is reachable at the new IP: bash ping 192.168.16.1

  2. Confirm static devices are reachable at their new addresses: bash ping <device-ip>

  3. Confirm DHCP clients receive addresses in the expected range: text 192.168.16.21 - 192.168.16.254

  4. Confirm Proxmox and OMV web interfaces are reachable from a LAN client.

  5. Check OPNsense ARP visibility: bash arp -a

  6. For DHCP devices, confirm they appear under: text OPNsense GUI -> Services -> DHCPv4 -> Leases

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• [ ] Update Proxmox static IP configuration to match the new LAN subnet.
  • [ ] Update OMV static IP configuration to match the new LAN subnet.
  • [ ] Verify all static devices use the correct gateway and DNS.
  • [ ] Reserve .2-.20 for infrastructure devices.
  • [ ] Keep .21-.254 for DHCP clients, or narrow the range if fewer DHCP addresses are needed.
  • [ ] Consider converting static devices to DHCP reservations in OPNsense.
  • [ ] Document the final IP allocation plan.
  • [ ] Check for other DHCP servers on the network to avoid conflicts.
  • [ ] Remove any temporary old-subnet aliases after migration is complete.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Changing the LAN subnet requires updating every static IP device.
  • DHCP lease tables only show DHCP clients, not manually configured static devices.
  • Static infrastructure devices should be kept outside the DHCP pool.
  • DHCP reservations are often easier to manage than configuring static IPs directly on every device.
  • When changing OPNsense LAN addressing, expect the management session to drop and reconnect using the new LAN IP.
  • IPv6 WAN tracking should only be enabled if the ISP provides IPv6 Prefix Delegation and IPv6 is intentionally being used.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ip addr show

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays network interfaces, assigned IP addresses, link state, and address scopes on Linux systems.

docs.title: Important arguments
docs.text: No extra arguments were used.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Used to verify the current IP address on OMV or another Linux-based device after the LAN subnet change.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text:
The device should show an address in the new subnet, such as:

192.168.16.x/24

docs.title: Failure indication
docs.text:
If the device still shows an address like:

192.168.1.x/24

then it is still configured for the old subnet and will likely be unreachable from the new LAN.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ip a

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Short form of ip addr show. Displays interface and IP address information.

docs.title: Important arguments
docs.text: No extra arguments were used.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Useful on Proxmox because the management IP is usually assigned to a Linux bridge such as vmbr0.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text:
The Proxmox bridge should show an address in the new subnet, such as:

192.168.16.x/24

docs.title: Failure indication
docs.text: If vmbr0 still has the old subnet, Proxmox management will not be reachable from the new LAN.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: arp -a

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays the ARP table, showing IP-to-MAC mappings for devices visible at Layer 2.

docs.title: Important arguments
docs.text: No extra arguments were used.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Used to check whether OPNsense or another host can see devices on the LAN even if those devices are not listed in DHCP leases.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Reachable LAN devices should appear with their IP and MAC address.

docs.title: Failure indication
docs.text: If Proxmox or OMV do not appear, they may be disconnected, on the wrong subnet, using the wrong VLAN, powered off, or otherwise unreachable at Layer 2/Layer 3.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ipconfig /release
ipconfig /renew

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Releases and renews a DHCP lease on Windows.

docs.title: Important arguments
docs.text:
• /release: drops the current DHCP lease.
  • /renew: requests a new DHCP lease.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Useful after changing the OPNsense LAN IP or DHCP range so Windows clients request an address from the new subnet.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text:
The Windows client receives an address in the new DHCP range, such as:

192.168.16.21 - 192.168.16.254

docs.title: Failure indication
docs.text: If the client gets an APIPA address like 169.254.x.x, it did not receive a DHCP response.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo dhclient -r && sudo dhclient

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Releases and renews a DHCP lease on many Linux systems using dhclient.

docs.title: Important arguments
docs.text:
• -r: releases the current lease.
  • &&: runs the second command only if the first succeeds.
  • dhclient: requests a new DHCP lease.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Useful for Linux clients that need to request a new IP from OPNsense after the LAN subnet change.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The client receives an IP in the new DHCP range.

docs.title: Failure indication
docs.text: If no lease is assigned, DHCP may be disabled, misconfigured, blocked, or the client may not be connected to the correct LAN.

docs.title: Likely Command Used
docs.text: ping 192.168.16.1

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Tests ICMP reachability to the OPNsense LAN gateway.

docs.title: Important arguments
docs.text: • 192.168.16.1: example OPNsense LAN IP after the subnet change.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Used to confirm that a client can reach the firewall on the new LAN address.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Successful replies indicate the gateway is reachable.

docs.title: Failure indication
docs.text: Failure may indicate: - Client is on the wrong subnet. - Client has incorrect gateway settings. - OPNsense LAN IP is configured differently. - Cabling, switch, VLAN, or interface assignment is wrong. - ICMP is blocked, though default LAN behavior usually allows this.

docs.title: Likely Command Used
docs.text: ping <client-ip>

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Tests reachability to another LAN device.

docs.title: Important arguments
docs.text: • <client-ip>: replace with the IP address of the device being tested.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Used to verify that Proxmox, OMV, or another LAN device is reachable after updating static IP settings.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Successful replies confirm basic network connectivity.

docs.title: Failure indication
docs.text: Failure may indicate: - Device has the wrong static IP. - Device has the wrong subnet mask. - Device has the wrong gateway. - Device is offline. - Device is on the wrong switch port or VLAN. - Host firewall blocks ICMP.

docs.title: OPNsense Console Action
docs.text: 2) Set interface IP address

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Runs the OPNsense console wizard for assigning or changing an interface IP address.

docs.title: Important prompts
docs.text: Typical prompts include: - Select interface, such as LAN. - Enter IPv4 address. - Enter subnet bit count, such as 24. - Configure upstream gateway, usually skipped for LAN. - Configure IPv6 via WAN tracking. - Configure DHCP range.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Used to change the LAN IP address when configuring the OPNsense LAN subnet.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: OPNsense applies the new LAN IP and optionally enables DHCP on that interface.

docs.title: Failure or risk
docs.text: Changing the LAN IP can immediately disconnect the current management session. The administrator must reconnect using the new LAN address.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Defines the pool of IPv4 addresses OPNsense can hand out to DHCP clients.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: This range allows DHCP clients to receive addresses while keeping .2-.20 available for manually assigned infrastructure devices.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: DHCP clients receive addresses from .21 through .254.

docs.title: Failure or risk
docs.text: If the range overlaps manually configured static IPs, duplicate IP conflicts can occur.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Defines an address block outside the DHCP pool for manually configured infrastructure devices.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: Useful for systems such as: - Proxmox hosts - OMV NAS - Access points - Printers - Switch management IPs - Other always-on infrastructure

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Static infrastructure devices do not conflict with DHCP clients.

docs.title: Failure or risk
docs.text: If a static device is accidentally configured inside the DHCP pool, OPNsense may hand the same IP to another device.

docs.title: Optional OPNsense Migration Approach: Temporary LAN Alias
Likely GUI Path
docs.text:
Interfaces -> Virtual IPs -> Settings

or depending on OPNsense version and configuration:

Interfaces -> LAN

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Adds an additional IP/subnet to the LAN interface so OPNsense can temporarily communicate with devices still on the old subnet.

docs.title: Why it may be used
docs.text: Useful when static devices are still configured for the old subnet and cannot be reached after the main LAN subnet changes.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Devices on both old and new subnets may be reachable long enough to migrate their static IP settings.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Do not leave temporary old-subnet aliases in place permanently unless intentionally designed. It can complicate routing, firewall policy, DNS assumptions, and troubleshooting.

docs.title: Configure an Additional OPT2 LAN in OPNsense
Summary
docs.text: This work session focused on adding a second internal LAN segment on an OPT2 interface in OPNsense. The goal was to assign a new interface, give it its own IPv4 subnet, optionally enable DHCP, allow traffic with firewall rules, and provide Internet access through outbound NAT when required.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Firewall/Router: OPNsense
  • Feature area: Interface assignment, LAN segmentation, DHCP, firewall policy, outbound NAT
  • Interface involved: OPT2 or an available unassigned NIC
  • Example subnet used in planning: 192.168.2.0/24
  • Example interface IP used in planning: 192.168.2.1/24

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: A new internal network needed to be created on another physical or logical interface in OPNsense so that devices connected to that segment could function as a separate LAN.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
No outage or error condition was recorded in this chat.

This was a configuration request, not a live troubleshooting session. The discussion did note one expected platform behavior:

  • New interfaces in OPNsense are typically blocked by default until firewall rules are added.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Reviewed the process to assign an available interface as OPT2 in OPNsense.
  2. Outlined enabling the new interface and configuring it with a static IPv4 address.
  3. Described optional DHCP server configuration for the new subnet.
  4. Added a basic allow rule on the OPT2 interface so clients on that network could pass traffic.
  5. Documented outbound NAT setup for Internet access if automatic behavior did not already cover the new subnet.

docs.title: Important UI flow
docs.text:
Assign the interface:

Interfaces -> Assignments

Purpose: Add and map an available NIC to OPT2.

Enable and configure the interface:

Interfaces -> OPT2

Purpose: Enable the interface and assign its static IPv4 address.

Optional DHCP configuration:

Services -> DHCPv4 -> OPT2

Purpose: Hand out IP addresses automatically to clients on the OPT2 subnet.

Firewall policy for OPT2:

Firewall -> Rules -> OPT2

Purpose: Allow traffic from the new LAN, since new interfaces are restricted by default.

Outbound NAT when needed:

Firewall -> NAT -> Outbound

Purpose: Ensure the new subnet is translated out the WAN for Internet access.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• Creating an additional LAN in OPNsense is not just an interface task; it usually requires work in four areas:
  • interface assignment
  • interface addressing
  • firewall rules
  • DHCP and/or NAT, depending on desired behavior
  • A newly assigned internal interface will not behave like an existing LAN until access rules are added.
  • A separate subnet must be assigned to the new interface. The example given was:
  • interface IP: 192.168.2.1/24
  • DHCP pool example: 192.168.2.100 - 192.168.2.200
  • Internet access for the new network may require explicit outbound NAT configuration if the firewall is not already handling the new subnet automatically.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
A reusable runbook was defined for bringing up an OPT2 LAN in OPNsense:

  1. Assign an unused interface.
  2. Enable it as OPT2.
  3. Give it a static IPv4 address on a new subnet.
  4. Enable DHCP if client auto-addressing is desired.
  5. Add at least one pass rule on OPT2.
  6. Add outbound NAT for the subnet if needed.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
No validation results were recorded in the chat.

Expected validation steps would be:

  • A client connected to the OPT2 network receives an IP address in the expected range.
  • The client uses the OPT2 interface IP as its gateway.
  • The client can reach allowed destinations according to firewall policy.
  • The client can reach the Internet if outbound NAT is correctly in place.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Confirm which physical NIC or VLAN-backed interface should be used for OPT2.
  • Verify whether outbound NAT is already automatic before creating manual rules.
