  • If multiple controllers appear: it may be possible to isolate the HDDs and Ceph SSD onto different controllers.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low risk. Read-only inspection command.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed. This is already a safe discovery command.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: ls -l /dev/disk/by-path/ | egrep 'ata-|sata'

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Lists persistent disk path symlinks and filters for SATA-attached devices. These path names often include the PCI controller address and ATA port mapping, which helps determine which controller a disk is attached to.

docs.title: Important flags and arguments
docs.text:
• ls -l Shows symlink targets rather than only file names.
  • /dev/disk/by-path/ Contains stable device symlinks based on bus/path topology.
  • egrep 'ata-|sata' Filters to show ATA/SATA-related entries.

docs.title: Why it was used at that moment
docs.text: It was used to map each SATA disk to its controller and to confirm whether the 4 HDDs and the Ceph SSD shared the same PCI SATA controller.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text:
Entries such as:

pci-0000:00:17.0-ata-1 -> ../../sda

These expose the PCI controller address and the Linux block device currently associated with that path.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: each disk can be mapped to a specific controller.
  • If all disks show the same PCI address: whole-controller passthrough would include them all.
  • If disks show different PCI addresses: controller separation may be possible.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low risk. Read-only inspection command.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed. This is an appropriate topology-inspection command.

docs.title: Likely command used
docs.text: qm set <VMID> -hostpci0 0000:00:17.0,pcie=1

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Adds a PCI passthrough device to a Proxmox VM configuration. In this case it would pass a SATA controller through to the guest.

docs.title: Important flags and arguments
docs.text:
• qm set Modifies a Proxmox VM configuration.
  • <VMID> Target VM ID, which would be the TrueNAS VM.
  • -hostpci0 Adds the first passthrough PCI device entry.
  • 0000:00:17.0 Full PCI address of the controller.
  • pcie=1 Presents the device as a PCIe device when appropriate.

docs.title: Why it was discussed
docs.text: This is the standard Proxmox method for passing an entire controller to a VM. It was relevant because the user initially wanted whole-controller passthrough for TrueNAS.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The VM configuration would include the SATA controller as a passthrough device, and on boot the guest OS would take ownership of that controller.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: the VM sees the controller and the host no longer manages disks behind it.
  • Failure or incorrect usage: the VM may fail to start, or the host may lose access to disks it still needs.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: High risk in this environment. Passing through 00:17.0 would also remove the Ceph SATA SSD and all other motherboard SATA disks from Proxmox host control.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Use a dedicated HBA for passthrough, or passthrough only specific disks by /dev/disk/by-id/.

docs.title: Likely command used
docs.text: lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,MODEL,SERIAL,TYPE

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Displays block devices with selected columns including size, model, serial, and type.

docs.title: Important flags and arguments
docs.text:
• lsblk Lists block devices.
  • -o Selects custom output columns.
  • NAME,SIZE,MODEL,SERIAL,TYPE Useful for distinguishing large HDDs from SSDs and identifying exact disks by model and serial.

docs.title: Why it was suggested
docs.text: It would be used to identify which Linux block devices correspond to the 16TB HDDs and which disk is the Ceph SSD.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A readable disk inventory showing device names and hardware identity data.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: disks can be clearly identified before passthrough planning.
  • Failure or missing serials: further identification may require SMART tools or additional queries.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low risk. Read-only inspection command.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Likely command used
docs.text: ls -l /dev/disk/by-id/ | egrep 'ata-|wwn-' | egrep -v 'part'

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Lists stable disk identifiers that should be used instead of /dev/sdX names when passing individual disks to a VM.

docs.title: Important flags and arguments
docs.text:
• /dev/disk/by-id/ Persistent device names based on serial/model/WWN.
  • egrep 'ata-|wwn-' Filters for ATA or WWN-based identifiers.
  • egrep -v 'part' Excludes partition symlinks and shows whole disks only.

docs.title: Why it was suggested
docs.text: This is the preferred identification method if passing through specific disks rather than a whole controller.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Stable device paths suitable for raw-disk passthrough in Proxmox.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: individual HDDs can be safely targeted by stable ID.
  • Failure or ambiguity: disk identification is incomplete and should be verified before passthrough.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low risk. Read-only inspection command.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Likely command used
docs.text: qm set <VMID> -scsi1 /dev/disk/by-id/ata-<disk-id>

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Assigns a host disk directly to a Proxmox VM as a raw SCSI device using a stable /dev/disk/by-id/ path.

docs.title: Important flags and arguments
docs.text:
• qm set Edits Proxmox VM configuration.
  • -scsi1 Attaches a disk to the first available SCSI slot.
  • /dev/disk/by-id/ata-<disk-id> Stable host path to the physical disk.

docs.title: Why it was discussed
docs.text: This is the fallback method when whole-controller passthrough is not possible and the user still wants TrueNAS to see real disks.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The specified physical disk becomes available inside the guest VM.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: the guest sees the individual disk.
  • Failure: incorrect device mapping or a VM boot/config issue.
  • Incorrect disk choice: a host-managed disk could be exposed accidentally.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Safe when done carefully with correct disk IDs, but dangerous if the wrong disk is selected.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Dedicated HBA passthrough remains safer and cleaner.

docs.title: Likely command used
docs.text: update-grub

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Rebuilds the GRUB bootloader configuration after changes to kernel boot parameters.

docs.title: Important flags and arguments
docs.text: No special flags here.

docs.title: Why it was discussed
docs.text: Needed when enabling IOMMU-related kernel parameters for PCI passthrough on Proxmox.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: GRUB config regenerates successfully.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: bootloader will use updated kernel arguments at next reboot.
  • Failure: passthrough-related boot parameter changes may not take effect.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Generally safe, but boot configuration changes should be made carefully on production hosts.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Back up /etc/default/grub before modifying it.

docs.title: Likely command used
docs.text: update-initramfs -u -k all

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Rebuilds initramfs images for installed kernels, ensuring boot-time modules and parameters are updated.

docs.title: Important flags and arguments
docs.text:
• -u Updates existing initramfs images.
  • -k all Applies the update to all installed kernels.

docs.title: Why it was discussed
docs.text: Often paired with IOMMU or vfio-related configuration changes before rebooting a Proxmox host for PCI passthrough readiness.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Initramfs images rebuild successfully.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success: boot environment is updated for passthrough-related changes.
  • Failure: the host may boot without necessary module/initramfs updates.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Common system administration command, but it affects boot preparation.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Ensure recent host backups and console access exist before major boot-chain changes.

docs.title: Likely command used
docs.text: find /sys/kernel/iommu_groups/ -type l | grep -E '00:17.0|03:00.0'

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Inspects IOMMU group membership for specific PCI devices to determine whether passthrough isolation is clean.

docs.title: Important flags and arguments
docs.text:
• find /sys/kernel/iommu_groups/ -type l Lists PCI devices grouped by IOMMU isolation boundaries.
  • grep -E '00:17.0|03:00.0' Filters for target controller/device addresses.

docs.title: Why it was discussed
docs.text: A device must be in a passthrough-safe IOMMU grouping, especially for full PCI controller passthrough.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Output showing the group containing the target controller.

docs.title: What success or failure would indicate
docs.text:
• Success with clean isolation: passthrough is more likely to be practical.
  • Shared group with critical devices: passthrough may be unsafe or require workarounds.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low risk. Read-only inspection command.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed.

docs.title: Concept note: IT mode
docs.text: No shell command was run for this, but it was an important technical point in the session.

docs.title: What it means
docs.text: IT mode means the HBA firmware exposes each attached drive directly to the operating system without hardware RAID abstraction.

docs.title: Why it matters here
docs.text: TrueNAS and ZFS should manage disks directly. An HBA in RAID/IR mode can hide or virtualize disks, which is not desirable for ZFS.

docs.title: Expected outcome
docs.text: With an HBA in IT mode: - each disk appears individually in TrueNAS - SMART and serial information are visible - ZFS manages redundancy and recovery directly

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Using a RAID-mode card instead of IT mode can complicate ZFS visibility, health checks, and recovery.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Buy an HBA confirmed to be flashed to IT mode before installation.

docs.title: VM 100 CPU Change Led Into HA/QMP/Ceph-Related Startup Failure
Summary
docs.text: VM 100 (debian-docker) was being adjusted to use 4 vCPUs. During the change, reset and stop/start attempts ran into HA-managed control flow, QMP socket timeouts, stale lock behavior, and a larger underlying Ceph health problem. The VM eventually became unable to start normally until Ceph health was restored.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Proxmox VE cluster
  • Host/node involved: mainframe
  • VM: 100 (debian-docker)
  • HA-managed VM resource at the start of the incident
  • Ceph cluster:
  • 5 monitors configured
  • mon.mainframe out of quorum
  • 5 OSDs up/in
  • slow ops on osd.0, osd.1, osd.2
  • VM storage backed by Ceph
  • Cloud-init attached to VM 100

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: A routine change to VM 100 CPU topology exposed multiple operational issues: 1. HA-controlled VM operations conflicted with manual qm commands. 2. QMP became unavailable during reset/stop attempts. 3. Ceph was degraded and blocking I/O. 4. VM startup later hung while generating the cloud-init ISO.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• CPU change was applied, but VM operations failed afterward.
  • Errors seen:
  • VM 100 qmp command 'system_reset' failed - got timeout
  • unable to connect to VM 100 qmp socket - timeout after 51 retries
  • Requesting HA stop for VM 100
  • can't lock file '/var/lock/qemu-server/lock-100.conf' - got timeout
  • Ceph health warnings:
  • 1/5 mons down
  • mon.mainframe ... is down (out of quorum)
  • 768 slow ops, oldest one blocked for ...
  • Later VM start failure:
  • generating cloud-init ISO
  • timeout waiting on systemd

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Changed VM 100 CPU topology.
qm set 100 --sockets 1 --cores 4

Purpose: set VM 100 to 4 vCPUs using 1 socket and 4 cores.

  1. Additional CPU topology changes were attempted while the VM was already unstable.
qm set 100 --sockets 1 --cores 8

Purpose: temporary adjustment while trying to recover VM startup.

  1. Attempted resets and stops from qm, which failed due to HA/QMP issues.
qm reset 100
qm stop 100
qm start 100

Purpose: try to restart the VM cleanly.

  1. Confirmed HA interactions and that qm start was being redirected through HA control.

  2. Investigated Ceph health.

ceph -s
ceph health detail

Purpose: determine whether storage health was preventing VM startup.

  1. Identified failed monitor and lagging OSD state on Ceph.

  2. Attempted monitor service recovery on mainframe.

systemctl status ceph-mon@mainframe --no-pager -l
timedatectl
systemctl restart ceph-mon@mainframe

Purpose: restore the failed monitor and rule out time-sync related quorum issues.

  1. Found that ceph-mon@mainframe was failed and systemd on mainframe later became unstable enough to return DBus errors.

  2. Protected Ceph during recovery.

ceph osd set noout
ceph mgr fail mainframe

Purpose: avoid rebalancing while the node was being recovered and move active manager duties away from the unhealthy node.

  1. Removed stale/broken mon.mainframe entry from the Ceph monmap.
ceph mon dump
ceph mon remove mainframe || ceph mon rm mainframe
ceph mon dump

Purpose: remove the dead monitor from cluster state so it could be recreated cleanly.

  1. Removed the old local monitor store and recreated the monitor on mainframe.
rm -rf /var/lib/ceph/mon/ceph-mainframe
pveceph mon create
systemctl start ceph-mon@mainframe
systemctl status ceph-mon@mainframe --no-pager -l

Purpose: rebuild the local Ceph monitor instance on mainframe.

  1. Verified cluster state after recovery.
ceph osd unset noout
ceph -s

Purpose: return Ceph to normal operation after the cluster was stable.

  1. Retried starting VM 100 once Ceph returned to healthy state.
qm start 100
qm status 100
  1. Observed cloud-init ISO generation timeout and later worked around cloud-init-related startup issues conceptually by considering removal of the CI drive for recovery.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The initial VM issue was not only a CPU configuration problem. It was compounded by:
  • HA-managed resource behavior
  • stuck/stale QMP state
  • Ceph monitor outage
  • Ceph slow ops on multiple OSDs
  • mon.mainframe had been removed from the monmap and needed to be recreated locally.
  • Once the Ceph monitor issue was addressed and noout was later unset, the cluster returned to:
  • HEALTH_OK
  • 33 PGs active+clean
  • The VM startup issue then shifted from storage health to cloud-init ISO generation timing.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The core storage-side outage was resolved by repairing Ceph rather than continuing to force VM restarts: - mon.mainframe was removed from the cluster map. - The local monitor store on mainframe was recreated. - Ceph recovered to HEALTH_OK. - VM 100 could run again, though cloud-init ISO generation remained a separate operational concern.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Successful validation included: - ceph -s showing: - HEALTH_OK - all OSDs up/in - PGs active+clean - ceph-mon@mainframe showing as active locally - VM 100 reaching status: running - VM 100 responding on the network:

ping -c1 192.168.16.3
nc -vz 192.168.16.3 22

SSH port 22 was reachable, confirming the guest was alive even though QEMU guest agent remained unavailable.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Revisit VM 100 CPU topology once the environment is stable and perform the change with a clean shutdown.
  • Investigate why cloud-init ISO generation timed out.
  • Stabilize QEMU guest agent on VM 100.
  • Review HA handling workflow for HA-managed VMs before issuing direct qm reset/stop commands.
  • Consider adding additional safeguards around Ceph monitor recovery and health checks.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• A VM startup failure on Proxmox can be a storage-cluster issue, not just a VM config issue.
  • HA-managed VMs should be handled through HA-aware workflows first.
  • QMP socket timeouts often indicate a deeper process, lock, or storage-state problem.
  • Ceph monitor recovery can be non-destructive when quorum still exists on other nodes.
  • noout is useful during controlled node recovery, but it must be unset afterward.

docs.title: Ceph Fully Stabilized and VM 100 Network Reachability Confirmed
Summary
docs.text: After the prior day’s Ceph repair work, the cluster was checked again and confirmed healthy. VM 100 was running, reachable by IP, and accepting TCP connections on port 22. The remaining issue shifted from storage failure to guest-side access and tooling reliability.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Proxmox VE host: mainframe
  • VM 100: debian-docker
  • Ceph cluster healthy
  • Guest IP observed: 192.168.16.3
  • QEMU Guest Agent not functioning inside the guest

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Even though VM 100 was running, the QEMU guest agent was not available, and SSH usability was still under question.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• qm status 100 reported the VM as running.
  • qm agent 100 ping returned:
  • QEMU guest agent is not running
  • Network tests from the Proxmox host succeeded:
  • ping worked
  • TCP 22 was open

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Verified VM state and attempted guest agent calls.
qm status 100
qm agent 100 ping
qm agent 100 network-get-interfaces

Purpose: confirm whether the guest was alive and whether QGA was functioning.

  1. Identified VM MAC from config.
qm config 100 | grep -E '^net0:'

Purpose: correlate guest networking if needed.

  1. Tested network reachability directly.
ping -c1 192.168.16.3
nc -vz 192.168.16.3 22

Purpose: verify whether the guest was reachable independently of QGA.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• VM 100 was alive and reachable on the network.
  • Port 22 was open, so the VM was not dead or storage-blocked at that point.
  • The remaining problem was likely guest-side SSH login/authentication and/or guest agent service availability rather than host networking or Proxmox storage.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: No final QEMU guest agent fix was completed in this session, but the critical state changed from “VM won’t start” to “VM is running and reachable.” The incident moved into guest-service troubleshooting rather than platform outage recovery.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• qm status 100 showed running
  • ICMP ping succeeded
  • nc -vz 192.168.16.3 22 reported SSH port open

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Repair qemu-guest-agent inside VM 100
  • Validate actual SSH login path, not just port reachability
  • Review guest service startup order after reboot
  • Review cloud-init impact on networking and SSH provisioning

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• QEMU guest agent absence does not mean the guest is down.
  • Network reachability tests are a better immediate truth source than agent state during recovery.
  • Separate platform health from guest-service health when troubleshooting.

docs.title: Gluetun VPN Stack Repaired for Deluge, qBittorrent, and SABnzbd
Summary
docs.text: A Docker VPN stack using Gluetun with Mullvad WireGuard was failing due to multiple configuration and state issues: a broken healthcheck assumption, invalid or missing WireGuard key handling, and a corrupted servers.json in the Gluetun config volume. The stack was repaired and brought back online. Remaining warnings pointed to CIFS/NAS write-permission or filename-handling issues on SABnzbd download paths.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• VM 100: Debian Docker VM
  • Docker Compose stack under /opt/compose/gluetun_stack/compose.yaml
  • Containers:
  • gluetun
  • deluge-vpn
  • qbittorrent-vpn
  • sabnzbd-vpn
  • VPN provider: Mullvad
  • VPN type: WireGuard
  • Shared Docker network: traefik-proxy
  • Reverse proxy: Traefik
  • App config directories under /opt/docker-apps
  • Download paths on NAS/CIFS mounts under /srv/remotemount/...

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The VPN-dependent Docker stack was unstable and unhealthy. Gluetun failed health checks and later crash-looped because of invalid VPN key handling and a corrupted server metadata file. Dependent containers were also being terminated when Gluetun restarted.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Gluetun marked unhealthy.
  • Earlier healthcheck was targeting 127.0.0.1:8000 without confirmed control server alignment.
  • Gluetun log errors included:
  • Wireguard settings: private key is not set
  • private key is not valid: ... illegal base64 data at input byte 0
  • ERROR reading servers from file: decoding servers: invalid character 'l' after object key:value pair
  • Dependent containers received shutdown signals:
  • Catching signal: SIGTERM
  • clean exits for Deluge and SABnzbd
  • qBittorrent exited with code 137
  • Later logs showed Gluetun healthy once repaired:
  • control server listening on :8000
  • healthcheck healthy
  • public IP resolved through Mullvad

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Reviewed the compose configuration for Gluetun and dependent containers.

  2. Identified that the healthcheck/control-server relationship needed to be aligned and that the stack needed a known-good Gluetun configuration.

  3. Updated the Gluetun service configuration to include:

  4. WireGuard with Mullvad
  5. inline private key in YAML
  6. control server on port 8000
  7. startup stability settings
  8. explicit healthcheck against /v1/health

  9. broad server selection by country rather than pinning a single hostname during recovery

  10. Brought the stack down and removed corrupted server metadata.

docker compose -f /opt/compose/gluetun_stack/compose.yaml down
rm -f /opt/docker-apps/Gluetun/config/servers.json /opt/docker-apps/Gluetun/config/servers.json.tmp 2>/dev/null || true

Purpose: fully stop the stack and delete the bad Gluetun server cache.

  1. Attempted ownership and permission repair on Gluetun config paths.
chown -R 1000:1000 /opt/docker-apps/Gluetun
chmod -R 775 /opt/docker-apps/Gluetun

Purpose: normalize host-side file ownership for the Gluetun config volume.

  1. Those ownership changes failed because they were run as non-root:

  2. Operation not permitted

  3. Started Gluetun alone for isolated validation.

docker compose -f /opt/compose/gluetun_stack/compose.yaml up -d gluetun
docker logs -f --tail=200 gluetun

Purpose: verify VPN connectivity and Gluetun health before starting dependent applications.

  1. Confirmed successful Gluetun startup:
  2. firewall enabled
  3. control server on port 8000
  4. healthcheck healthy
  5. Mullvad WireGuard connected

  6. public IP reported in Canada

  7. Started Deluge, qBittorrent, and SABnzbd after Gluetun stabilized.

docker compose -f /opt/compose/gluetun_stack/compose.yaml up -d deluge qbittorrent sabnzbd

Purpose: restore the full VPN stack after the shared network namespace anchor was healthy.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The corrupted /gluetun/servers.json was a hard blocker and caused repeated early exits.
  • WireGuard key handling was briefly incorrect during configuration changes.
  • Keeping the private key inline in YAML was workable as long as the value was quoted and valid.
  • Once Gluetun stabilized, Deluge, qBittorrent, and SABnzbd all launched successfully through the shared namespace.
  • qBittorrent WebUI came up on port 8880.
  • Deluge daemon and Web UI started successfully.
  • SABnzbd started successfully but warned that:
  • /usenet-downloads/incomplete was not writable with special character filenames
  • /usenet-downloads/complete was not writable with special character filenames

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
The Gluetun stack was successfully restored by: - deleting the corrupted servers.json - correcting WireGuard key handling - using a stable Gluetun configuration - bringing up Gluetun by itself first - starting dependent containers only after Gluetun became healthy

The stack was operational at the end of the session.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Validation came from: - Gluetun logs showing: - firewall enabled - healthcheck healthy - DNS ready - public VPN IP in Canada - qBittorrent logs showing WebUI startup on localhost:8880 - Deluge logs showing daemon and UI availability - SABnzbd logs showing full service startup on port 8088

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Fix SABnzbd download path writeability and special-character handling on NAS/CIFS mounts.
  • Revisit whether DNS-over-TLS should remain enabled or temporarily disabled for stability.
  • Run host-side permission changes with sudo if ownership normalization is still desired.
  • Consider whether to repin to a specific Mullvad hostname only after long-term stability is confirmed.
  • Rotate WireGuard private keys if any prior testing exposed or mishandled them.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Start Gluetun by itself first when debugging a VPN-dependent shared-namespace stack.
  • A corrupted persistent metadata file can look like a networking problem when it is actually a storage/config-volume problem.
  • When Gluetun is the network namespace owner, every dependent container inherits its lifecycle.
  • Non-root host users cannot repair ownership on protected bind-mounted config paths.
  • CIFS/NAS mount behavior can surface as application warnings even after the VPN stack itself is healthy.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm set 100 --sockets 1 --cores 4

What it does: Sets VM 100 CPU topology to 1 socket and 4 cores. Important arguments: - 100: VM ID - --sockets 1: one CPU socket exposed to the guest - --cores 4: four cores per socket Why it was used: To change VM 100 to 4 vCPUs. Expected result: VM config reflects 4 total vCPUs. Success indicates: The config change was accepted. Failure indicates: Proxmox config write or locking issue.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm set 100 --sockets 1 --cores 8

What it does: Changes VM CPU topology to 8 cores temporarily. Why it was used: Recovery/testing while the VM was already unstable. Risk: Changing CPU topology during instability can complicate troubleshooting.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm reset 100

What it does: Issues a hard reset to the VM through QMP. Why it was used: To force a reboot when the guest was not responding normally. Expected result: Immediate guest reset. Failure indicates: QMP unavailable, stuck VM process, or host-side control path problem. Risk: Equivalent to pulling power inside the guest.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm stop 100

What it does: Stops the VM from the Proxmox host. Why it was used: To halt the guest during recovery. Expected result: VM transitions to stopped. Failure indicates: lock issue, HA interaction, or QMP/control-path problem.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm start 100

What it does: Starts VM 100. Why it was used: To test whether the platform and storage were healthy enough to boot the guest. Expected result: VM enters running state. Failure indicates: storage, config, HA, lock, or runtime startup problem.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ha-manager status

What it does: Shows Proxmox HA resource status. Why it was used: To determine whether VM 100 was HA-managed and whether HA was interfering with direct VM control. Expected result: Resource state and node placement information.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ceph -s

What it does: Displays a concise Ceph cluster status summary. Why it was used: To determine whether storage cluster health was blocking VM operations. Expected result: cluster health, mon/mgr/osd state, PG state, and usage summary. Success indicates: cluster responds and provides current health. Failure indicates: cluster connectivity or CLI environment issue.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ceph health detail

What it does: Shows detailed Ceph health warnings and errors. Why it was used: To identify specific monitor and OSD problems. Expected result: exact warning classes such as MON_DOWN or SLOW_OPS.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
systemctl status ceph-mon@mainframe --no-pager -l

What it does: Shows detailed service status for the Ceph monitor on mainframe. Important arguments: - --no-pager: prints directly to the terminal - -l: full lines without truncation Why it was used: To diagnose why mon.mainframe was out of quorum. Expected result: active/running or failed state with log excerpts.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
timedatectl

What it does: Displays local time, time sync, and NTP status. Why it was used: Time skew can break Ceph monitor quorum and elections. Expected result: synchronized system clock.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ceph osd set noout

What it does: Sets the Ceph noout flag to prevent OSDs being marked out automatically. Why it was used: To avoid cluster rebalance while recovering a node. Expected result: cluster health mentions noout. Risk: Should be unset after maintenance; leaving it enabled can hide real failures.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ceph mgr fail mainframe

What it does: Forces the active Ceph manager on mainframe to fail over to a standby manager. Why it was used: To reduce dependency on the unhealthy node during recovery. Expected result: another mgr becomes active.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ceph mon dump

What it does: Dumps the current monitor map. Why it was used: To verify whether mon.mainframe was still registered in the cluster. Expected result: monitor addresses and monmap epoch.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ceph mon remove mainframe || ceph mon rm mainframe

What it does: Removes mon.mainframe from the Ceph monitor map. Why it was used: To cleanly remove the stale/broken monitor definition before recreating it. Expected result: mainframe no longer appears in ceph mon dump. Risk: Safe only because the remaining monitors already had quorum.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
rm -rf /var/lib/ceph/mon/ceph-mainframe

What it does: Deletes the local monitor data directory for mainframe. Why it was used: To remove the broken local monitor store before recreating it. Expected result: directory removed so a clean monitor can be created. Risk: Destructive to the local monitor instance; appropriate only when recreating the monitor and cluster quorum already exists elsewhere.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
pveceph mon create

What it does: Creates a Ceph monitor on the local Proxmox node. Why it was used: To recreate mon.mainframe after removing the stale entry and local data. Expected result: local monitor service and data store are rebuilt.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ceph osd unset noout

What it does: Removes the Ceph noout maintenance flag. Why it was used: To return the cluster to normal behavior after recovery. Expected result: cluster health no longer reports noout.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm status 100

What it does: Shows whether VM 100 is running or stopped. Why it was used: To validate guest state during recovery.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm agent 100 ping

What it does: Tests connectivity to the QEMU Guest Agent inside VM 100. Why it was used: To check whether the guest agent was functioning. Expected result: a successful ping response. Failure indicates: guest agent not running, missing device, or guest-side service issue.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm agent 100 network-get-interfaces

What it does: Queries guest network interfaces through QEMU Guest Agent. Why it was used: To discover the guest IP from Proxmox without logging into the VM. Failure indicates: guest agent not available.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
qm config 100 | grep -E '^net0:'

What it does: Extracts the VM’s primary network interface definition from config. Why it was used: To identify the MAC address for ARP/neighbor lookup.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ping -c1 192.168.16.3

What it does: Sends one ICMP echo request to the guest IP. Why it was used: To confirm guest network reachability. Success indicates: the guest responds on the network. Failure indicates: guest networking, firewall, or host routing issue.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
nc -vz 192.168.16.3 22

What it does: Tests TCP connectivity to port 22 on the guest. Important arguments: - -v: verbose - -z: scan mode without sending payload Why it was used: To check whether SSH was listening. Success indicates: TCP port 22 open. Failure indicates: SSH not listening, filtering, or routing issue.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker compose -f /opt/compose/gluetun_stack/compose.yaml down

What it does: Stops and removes the services in the Gluetun stack. Why it was used: To fully reset the VPN stack during repair. Expected result: containers removed cleanly.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
rm -f /opt/docker-apps/Gluetun/config/servers.json /opt/docker-apps/Gluetun/config/servers.json.tmp 2>/dev/null || true

What it does: Removes Gluetun’s cached server metadata files. Why it was used: The file was corrupted and causing Gluetun startup failure. Expected result: Gluetun regenerates a valid servers.json on next startup.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
chown -R 1000:1000 /opt/docker-apps/Gluetun

What it does: Recursively changes ownership of the Gluetun config directory. Why it was used: To align host file ownership with the container’s runtime UID/GID. Expected result: Gluetun can read/write config files as user 1000. Failure indicates: insufficient privileges or filesystem restrictions. Risk: Recursive ownership changes should be used carefully on shared paths.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
chmod -R 775 /opt/docker-apps/Gluetun

What it does: Recursively sets directory/file permissions to group-writable/readable/executable as appropriate. Why it was used: To ensure Gluetun could write its config cache and state. Failure indicates: insufficient privileges or filesystem restrictions.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker compose -f /opt/compose/gluetun_stack/compose.yaml up -d gluetun

What it does: Starts only the Gluetun service in detached mode. Why it was used: To validate the VPN container independently before starting dependent apps. Expected result: Gluetun reaches healthy state and remains running.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker logs -f --tail=200 gluetun

What it does: Follows the most recent 200 lines of Gluetun logs. Why it was used: To monitor startup, VPN initialization, DNS, healthcheck, and exit reasons in real time.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker compose -f /opt/compose/gluetun_stack/compose.yaml up -d deluge qbittorrent sabnzbd

What it does: Starts the VPN-dependent application containers after Gluetun is healthy. Why it was used: To restore the rest of the stack once the shared network namespace owner was stable.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker ps --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}'

What it does: Shows running containers with names, status, and published ports in table form. Why it was used: To quickly verify container state after recovery.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/health

What it does: Queries the Gluetun control/health endpoint. Important arguments: - -f: fail on HTTP errors - -sS: quiet normal output but show errors Why it was used: To confirm Gluetun health from inside the host/container namespace. Expected result: successful HTTP response.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
docker exec -it gluetun sh -c 'wg show; wget -qO- https://ipinfo.io/ip || curl -s https://ipinfo.io/ip'

What it does: Runs WireGuard status and public IP checks inside the Gluetun container. Why it was used: To verify VPN tunnel state and the public egress IP.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo chown -R 1000:1000 /opt/docker-apps/{Deluge,qBittorrent,SABnzbd}

Likely command used. What it does: Corrects ownership of LinuxServer.io app config directories. Why it was recommended: Those containers run as UID/GID 1000. Expected result: apps can read/write their config and state correctly.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo chmod -R 775 /opt/docker-apps/{Deluge,qBittorrent,SABnzbd}

Likely command used. What it does: Applies writable permissions to the app config directories. Why it was recommended: To reduce config-write failures for LinuxServer.io containers.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo mount -a

Likely command used. What it does: Mounts all filesystems listed in /etc/fstab that are not already mounted. Why it was recommended: To apply corrected CIFS mount options for NAS-backed download directories. Expected result: updated mount options become active without full reboot. Risk: A bad /etc/fstab entry can cause mount failures or boot problems later if not validated carefully.

docs.title: Recover VM 100 from Emergency Mode with a Helper VM
Summary
docs.text: VM 100 entered emergency mode again. A helper VM workflow was used to attach VM 100’s disk, inspect the root filesystem offline, and repair configuration issues that were preventing normal boot.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Proxmox VE
  • VM 100: Debian-based Docker VM
  • Helper VM: VM 101
  • Storage:
  • cephpool:vm-100-disk-0
  • prior failed assumption of local-lvm:vm-100-disk-0
  • Guest filesystem:
  • ext4 root partition
  • EFI partition present
  • Access methods discussed:
  • Proxmox VNC/console
  • SSH
  • serial console concepts

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: VM 100 failed to boot normally and dropped into emergency mode.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• VM 100 entered emergency mode again.
  • Initial attempt to attach VM 100’s disk to the helper VM failed with:
  • no such logical volume pve/vm-100-disk-0
  • Hostname confusion occurred later, making the active VM appear to be helper-vm.
  • Password/login recovery work was also needed after the filesystem work.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Attempted to attach VM 100’s disk to helper VM 101 using an incorrect storage target.
  2. Corrected the disk attachment to Ceph-backed storage.
  3. Installed and enabled SSH server on the helper VM so commands could be pasted more easily than through VNC.
  4. Used lsblk to identify the attached disk and partitions.
  5. Determined that the attached VM 100 root filesystem was on /dev/sda1 in the helper VM context.
  6. Mounted the root filesystem and later bind-mounted /dev, /proc, /sys, and /run for chroot access.
  7. Entered a chroot to repair login-related configuration and user access.

Important commands used:

qm set 101 -scsi1 cephpool:vm-100-disk-0

Attach VM 100’s Ceph-backed disk to helper VM 101.

lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,FSTYPE,TYPE,MOUNTPOINT

Identify the attached disk and its partitions.

sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/vm100
for d in dev proc sys run; do sudo mount --bind /$d /mnt/vm100/$d; done
sudo chroot /mnt/vm100 /bin/bash

Mount the offline root filesystem and enter it for repair.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The VM 100 disk was not on local-lvm; it was on cephpool.
  • The helper VM saw:
  • its own disk as sdb
  • VM 100’s root disk as sda
  • The actual root filesystem was on the partition /dev/sda1, not the whole disk device.
  • Offline repair through chroot was possible once the correct partition was mounted.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The helper VM workflow succeeded once the correct Ceph disk was attached and the correct root partition was mounted.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• lsblk showed a normal Linux filesystem hierarchy after mounting /dev/sda1.
  • Chroot launched successfully:
  • root@helper-vm:/#

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Detach VM 100’s disk from the helper VM after repairs.
  • Document the correct Ceph-backed helper VM procedure.
  • Keep a known-good helper VM with SSH enabled for future recovery.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Always verify the VM disk’s real Proxmox storage backend before attaching it to a helper VM.
  • In helper VM recovery work, expect to mount a partition such as /dev/sdX1, not the parent disk.
  • SSH access to the helper VM is much easier than relying on pasted commands in VNC.

docs.title: Restore Console and User Access on VM 100
Summary
docs.text: After offline access to VM 100 was obtained, login recovery work focused on restoring usable local credentials and removing confusion caused by broken or partial recovery attempts.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• VM 100 Debian guest
  • Helper VM chroot into VM 100 root filesystem
  • Users involved:
  • debian
  • rescue
  • SSH configuration file:
  • /etc/ssh/sshd_config

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: The expected password was no longer working for console login, and earlier password reset attempts did not succeed.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Passwords were not working at the console.
  • qm set 100 -ciuser debian -cipassword ... did not solve the issue.
  • Earlier chroot attempts were interrupted or mangled.
  • One repair attempt showed:
  • passwd: user 'debian' does not exist
  • chpasswd: (user debian) pam_chauthtok() failed
  • Another attempt showed:
  • useradd: command not found
  • A pasted command corrupted the SSH config update line.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Determined that cloud-init password injection was not a reliable fix for the current state.
  2. Entered chroot again, this time with the correct bind mounts and a fixed PATH.
  3. Removed /etc/nologin if present.
  4. Ensured the sudo group existed.
  5. Created or reset a rescue account.
  6. Reset the debian password.
  7. Corrected SSH password authentication directives in sshd_config.

Important commands used:

export PATH=/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

Restore admin tools inside the chroot environment.

rm -f /etc/nologin || true
getent group sudo >/dev/null || groupadd sudo

Ensure local logins are not blocked and sudo group exists.

id -u rescue >/dev/null 2>&1 || /usr/sbin/useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G sudo rescue
echo 'rescue:Docker123' | /usr/sbin/chpasswd

id -u debian >/dev/null 2>&1 || /usr/sbin/useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G sudo debian
echo 'debian:Docker123' | /usr/sbin/chpasswd

Create/reset local administrative accounts.

sed -i -E 's/^\s*#?\s*PasswordAuthentication\s+.*/PasswordAuthentication yes/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sed -i -E 's/^\s*#?\s*(KbdInteractiveAuthentication|ChallengeResponseAuthentication)\s+.*/KbdInteractiveAuthentication yes/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
/usr/sbin/sshd -t

Repair SSH password-auth directives and validate the SSH config syntax.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The earlier cloud-init password approach was not the right recovery path.
  • The chroot environment initially lacked a working admin PATH, which caused useradd not to be found.
  • Pasted multi-line commands could corrupt config-editing lines if entered carelessly.
  • Once the chroot environment was prepared correctly, account repair succeeded.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: A clean chroot session with a proper PATH was used to reset the local login environment and restore administrative access.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• chpasswd succeeded for both rescue and debian.
  • sshd -t passed.
  • The chroot exit completed cleanly after the account work.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Log into VM 100 using the restored account and immediately set a known-good permanent password.
  • Remove the temporary rescue account once stable access is confirmed.
  • Review whether cloud-init should continue to manage login credentials for this VM.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• For broken Linux guests, offline chroot repair is often more reliable than trying to force cloud-init password changes.
  • Always set a valid PATH in rescue/chroot sessions.
  • Test SSH configuration with sshd -t before rebooting or relying on it.

docs.title: Diagnose Hostname Confusion and Cloud-Init Behavior on VM 100
Summary
docs.text: During recovery, VM 100 appeared to rename itself to helper-vm. Investigation showed cloud-init was still active and the system hostname state was inconsistent.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• VM 100 Debian guest
  • cloud-init enabled
  • Files examined:
  • /etc/hostname
  • /etc/hosts
  • /etc/cloud/cloud.cfg
  • /etc/cloud/cloud.cfg.d/*

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: VM 100 appeared to be running with the wrong hostname.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• hostnamectl reported:
  • Static hostname: helper-vm
  • /etc/hostname contained helper-vm
  • cloud-init logs showed it was active on boot.
  • preserve_hostname: false was set.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Checked hostname status and local hostname files.
  2. Queried cloud-init state and logs.
  3. Considered using cloud-init clean + reapplying cicustom data later.

Important commands used:

hostnamectl
cat /etc/hostname
sed -n '1,40p' /etc/hosts

Inspect current hostname state.

sudo journalctl -b | grep -Ei 'set hostname|cloud-ini|dhcp|dhclient|networkd.*hostname'
systemctl is-enabled cloud-init || true
cloud-init status || true
grep -i preserve_hostname /etc/cloud/cloud.cfg || true
ls -1 /etc/cloud/cloud.cfg.d/ || true

Check cloud-init activity and hostname-related behavior.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• cloud-init was still enabled and had run on boot.
  • preserve_hostname: false meant cloud-init was allowed to rewrite hostname state.
  • The VM identity and hostname state had become inconsistent during recovery work.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: No final hostname remediation was completed in this session, but the cause was narrowed to cloud-init-managed hostname behavior.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Hostname drift was confirmed through both hostnamectl and file inspection.
  • cloud-init’s active role was confirmed through logs and status.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Decide whether to disable cloud-init after the VM is rebuilt or stabilized.
  • Reassert the intended hostname through the VM’s cloud-init data or guest config.
  • Verify machine identity and cloud-init seed behavior.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Cloud-init can reapply identity state during recovery if left enabled.
  • Hostname anomalies after clone/helper workflows should always prompt a cloud-init review.

docs.title: Diagnose Docker Mount and Bind Issues on VM 100
Summary
docs.text: After basic guest recovery, attention shifted to Docker storage paths. /opt/compose and /opt/docker-apps were expected to be backed by /var/lib/docker/compose and /var/lib/docker/appdata, but the mount state and bind direction were inconsistent.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• VM 100 Debian Docker VM
  • Docker root on /var/lib/docker
  • Expected bind layout:
  • /var/lib/docker/appdata
  • /var/lib/docker/compose
  • /opt/docker-apps
  • /opt/compose
  • systemd unit:
  • docker-verify.mountguard.service

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Docker-related bind mounts were not behaving as expected, and /opt/compose appeared empty.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• findmnt initially showed no active mounts for the expected paths.
  • /etc/fstab did not contain the /var/lib/docker mount entry at one point.
  • docker-verify.mountguard.service did not start because:
  • ConditionPathIsMountPoint=/var/lib/docker was not met
  • docker.service still ran even though /var/lib/docker was not a mountpoint.
  • /opt/compose appeared empty.
  • Bind test files proved the bind relationship worked only after repair.
  • Attempts to inspect mounted paths without root produced Permission denied.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Checked active mount state.
  2. Reviewed current /etc/fstab.
  3. Repaired the guard script typo.
  4. Added missing /var/lib/docker and bind mount entries.
  5. Disabled the guard temporarily when it blocked startup.
  6. Wrote a new Docker systemd override.
  7. Verified bind behavior with test files.

Important commands used:

findmnt /var/lib/docker /var/lib/docker/appdata /var/lib/docker/compose || true
grep -Ev '^\s*#|^\s*$' /etc/fstab | sed -n '1,200p'
systemctl status docker-verify.mountguard.service --no-pager || true
systemctl status docker --no-pager || true

Check mount state, fstab contents, and Docker/guard unit status.

sudo mount -a
findmnt /var/lib/docker

Apply fstab mounts and verify Docker root state.

sudo bash -lc 'touch /opt/compose/.bindtest && ls -la /var/lib/docker/compose | grep bindtest || echo "compose bind not active"'
sudo bash -lc 'touch /opt/docker-apps/.bindtest && ls -la /var/lib/docker/appdata | grep bindtest || echo "appdata bind not active"'

Prove bind relationships by creating test files on one side and reading them on the other.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• /var/lib/docker was at one point just part of the root filesystem, not a dedicated mount.
  • The guard unit correctly refused to run when /var/lib/docker was not a mountpoint.
  • A malformed or incomplete fstab and guard script contributed to confusion.
  • The bind directions had to be carefully reviewed to match the intended layout.
  • /opt/compose was functionally empty except for .bindtest and a small dockge directory.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The bind relationships were brought back into a working state for the current session, but the absence of expected compose content suggested that the compose files were either elsewhere or already lost.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• docker info reported Root=/var/lib/docker.
  • Bind tests showed .bindtest on both sides of the bind.
  • df -h /var/lib/docker showed the filesystem in use.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Confirm the intended canonical path direction and keep it consistent.
  • Remove temporary .bindtest files.
  • Revisit whether the guard should be re-enabled after the rebuild.
  • Rebuild missing compose directories from backup or prior exports.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• A working Docker daemon does not prove the Docker data disk is mounted correctly.
  • findmnt and test files are the fastest way to prove whether a bind mount is real.
  • systemd guard units are useful, but only when the underlying mount logic is correct.

docs.title: Restore NAS CIFS Mount Reliability and Symlink Support
Summary
docs.text: A CIFS-mounted NAS share was needed for backups, but backups failed with CIFS timeouts and symlink-related errors. The NAS mount was rebuilt with a clean credentials file and proper mount options, including mfsymlinks.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• Debian guest VM
  • NAS share:
  • //192.168.16.21/Media
  • Mountpoint:
  • /srv/remotemount/NAS
  • Credentials file:
  • /root/.smbcredentials
  • systemd-generated units from /etc/fstab:
  • srv-remotemount-NAS.automount
  • srv-remotemount-NAS.mount

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Backups to the NAS failed because the share had both connectivity issues and symlink incompatibilities.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• rsync errors like:
  • Input/output error (5) on symlink creation
  • kernel logs:
  • CIFS: VFS: \\192.168.16.21 has not responded in 180 seconds. Reconnecting...
  • Duplicate fstab entries caused:
  • Duplicate entry in '/etc/fstab'?
  • Systemd initially could not show the generated units until the fstab state was corrected.

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Confirmed the mount lacked the right options.
  2. Created a root-only SMB credentials file.
  3. Mounted the share manually with mfsymlinks.
  4. Verified read/write access, ownership mapping, and symlink behavior.
  5. Added a single canonical CIFS automount line to /etc/fstab.
  6. Removed duplicate fstab entries and regenerated systemd units.
  7. Verified the generated automount and mount units were active.

Important commands used:

sudo install -m 600 -o root -g root /dev/null /root/.smbcredentials
sudo bash -lc 'cat > /root/.smbcredentials <<EOF
username=admin
password=Kingsley1
# domain=WORKGROUP
EOF'

Create a protected SMB credentials file for CIFS mounts.

sudo mount -t cifs //192.168.16.21/Media /srv/remotemount/NAS \
  -o rw,vers=3.1.1,credentials=/root/.smbcredentials,uid=1000,gid=1000,iocharset=utf8,mfsymlinks,noperm,noserverino,dir_mode=0775,file_mode=0664,actimeo=1,cache=none

Mount the NAS manually with symlink emulation and explicit ownership mapping.

ln -s target.txt /srv/remotemount/NAS/_cifs_test/link-to-target
readlink /srv/remotemount/NAS/_cifs_test/link-to-target
cat /srv/remotemount/NAS/_cifs_test/link-to-target

Validate that symlink behavior works over CIFS.

systemctl list-units 'srv-remotemount-NAS.*' --all
systemctl status srv-remotemount-NAS.automount srv-remotemount-NAS.mount

Validate the fstab-generated automount units.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• mfsymlinks was necessary for Linux symlink behavior over SMB.
  • The share could be mounted with expected ownership as UID/GID 1000.
  • Duplicate fstab lines caused systemd generator conflicts.
  • Once cleaned up, the automount worked normally.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: The NAS mount was rebuilt using a dedicated credentials file and a single correct /etc/fstab entry with systemd automount support and mfsymlinks.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Files could be created on the share as debian:debian.
  • Symlink creation and dereferencing worked.
  • srv-remotemount-NAS.automount and srv-remotemount-NAS.mount both appeared as loaded/generated and active.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Consider increasing the idle timeout from 60 seconds if reconnects remain annoying.
  • Monitor for further CIFS reconnect events.
  • Reuse the same mount pattern for other shares if needed.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• CIFS path issues and symlink issues should be solved before running bulk rsync jobs.
  • Keep only one canonical fstab entry per mountpoint.
  • systemd automounts are reliable once the generator input is clean.

docs.title: Backup Docker Appdata and Compose to NAS
Summary
docs.text: The goal shifted from fixing VM 100 in place to preserving Docker state for a future rebuild. Backups of /opt/docker-apps and /opt/compose were attempted to the NAS.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• VM 100 (debian-docker)
  • Docker appdata:
  • /opt/docker-apps
  • Compose directory:
  • /opt/compose
  • NAS target:
  • /srv/remotemount/NAS/Backups/docker-VM

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: A full backup of Docker appdata and compose needed to be created before rebuilding the VM.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Initial backup attempts using more complex approaches led to confusion.
  • /opt/compose appeared nearly empty:
  • .bindtest
  • dockge
  • Initial snapshot verification showed:
  • appdata source about 811 MB
  • backup snapshot about 779 MB
  • compose source 8 KB
  • compose snapshot effectively empty
  • CIFS read-ahead messages appeared during copy:
  • CIFS: __readahead_batch() returned 257/1024

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Determined the canonical live source directories were:
  2. /opt/docker-apps
  3. /opt/compose
  4. Created backup roots on the NAS.
  5. Created an rsync exclude file for logs, caches, and temp data.
  6. Created timestamped snapshots using rsync.
  7. Performed size and file-count comparisons.
  8. Spot-checked large files to confirm they existed in the backup.

Important commands used:

APPDATA_SRC="/opt/docker-apps"
COMPOSE_SRC="/opt/compose"

Set the actual source paths for backup.

TS="$(date +%F_%H%M%S)"
DEST_APP="$BASE/appdata/snap-$TS"
DEST_CMP="$BASE/compose/snap-$TS"
mkdir -p "$DEST_APP" "$DEST_CMP"

rsync -aHAX --delete --numeric-ids $EXC "$APPDATA_SRC"/ "$DEST_APP"/
rsync -aHAX --delete --numeric-ids $EXC "$COMPOSE_SRC"/ "$DEST_CMP"/

Create snapshot-style backups of appdata and compose.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The live compose directory on the VM was effectively empty except for minimal content.
  • Appdata backup largely succeeded; missing items were later traced to exclusions and SMB restore complexity.
  • The small compose snapshot was real, not just a display artifact.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: A usable appdata backup path was established on the NAS. Compose recovery remained unresolved because the source compose directory was mostly empty.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Appdata snapshots existed on the NAS with many expected app directories.
  • Spot checks confirmed large appdata files existed in the snapshot.
  • Size and file-count comparisons were close enough to show the backup was mostly successful.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Rebuild missing compose files from prior exports, backups, or previous chats.
  • Consider archive-based backups instead of raw rsync to CIFS.
  • Add verification steps to future backup jobs.

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Appdata and compose should be backed up separately and verified separately.
  • A nearly empty compose tree should be treated as a real recovery problem, not just a mount bug.
  • Raw rsync to CIFS works, but archive-first workflows are safer for large restores.

docs.title: Restore Appdata from Offen Backup Archive
Summary
docs.text: A large Offen backup archive was restored back into /opt/docker-apps after missing files were discovered in the earlier NAS snapshot. The recovery focused on restoring all appdata content from a tar.gz archive.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
• VM 100 (debian-docker)
  • Backup source:
  • /srv/remotemount/NAS/Tools/Backups/Docker/offen/backup-[date removed].tar.gz
  • Restore target:
  • /opt/docker-apps
  • Temporary extraction paths:
  • /tmp/offen-restore-*
  • /var/tmp/offen-restore
  • Backup tool used originally:
  • Offen Docker Volume Backup

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Files were still missing after earlier backup and restore work. A complete restore from the older Offen archive was needed, and the archive layout was initially misunderstood.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• Early restore attempts failed because:
  • the wrong .tar versus .tar.gz extension was assumed
  • archive extraction commands relied on lost shell variables after logout
  • restore attempts incorrectly targeted /opt/docker-apps/Offen
  • One tar attempt failed with:
  • tar (child): /archive.tar.gz: Cannot open: No such file or directory
  • Another tar path attempt failed with:
  • tar: backup/my-app-backup: Not found in archive
  • The archive repeatedly showed:
  • tar: Removing leading '/' from member names
  • CIFS messages appeared during copy:
  • CIFS: __readahead_batch() returned 257/1024

docs.title: Actions Taken
docs.text:
1. Confirmed the archive was really a .tar.gz.
  2. Verified the archive with gzip -t.
  3. Listed archive contents with tar -tzf.
  4. Confirmed the archive root structure was:
  5. /backup/my-app-backup/...
  6. Copied the archive locally before extraction to avoid CIFS issues.
  7. Extracted the payload into /opt/docker-apps using strip logic.
  8. Observed that some content first landed under:
  9. /opt/docker-apps/backup/my-app-backup
  10. Moved payload contents up into /opt/docker-apps.
  11. Used a staging workflow and later re-extracted the entire payload directly into /opt/docker-apps.
  12. Considered overwrite semantics to restore deleted JSON, YAML, and DB files.

Important commands used:

gzip -t "$TMP/archive.tar.gz" && echo "gzip OK"
tar -tzf "$TMP/archive.tar.gz" | head -20

Validate archive integrity and inspect internal path layout.

sudo tar -xzpf "$TMP/archive.tar.gz" \
  -C "$DEST" \
  --strip-components=2 \
  /backup/my-app-backup

Extract the backup payload into the appdata root while dropping the wrapper path.

sudo rsync -a --remove-source-files /opt/docker-apps/backup/my-app-backup/ /opt/docker-apps/

Move restored payload contents up one level after an earlier wrapper-path extraction.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
• The backup archive was valid.
  • The archive layout used an absolute top-level path:
  • /backup/my-app-backup/...
  • The restore destination was the full /opt/docker-apps tree, not an Offen subdirectory.
  • Missing files could still occur if they were not in the specific backup date being restored, or if later confusion about destination structure hid them.

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text: A full appdata restore from the Offen archive into /opt/docker-apps was completed. The restored tree showed many expected application directories, including: - Radarr-ES - Monica - Overseerr - Plex - Traefik - Notifiarr - and many others

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• gzip -t returned gzip OK.
  • tar -tzf showed expected app directories under /backup/my-app-backup.
  • du -sh /opt/docker-apps returned 45 GB after restore.
  • find /opt/docker-apps -maxdepth 2 -type d showed many recovered application directories.

docs.title: Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
• Confirm whether all expected per-app files are present by checking specific known-missing JSON, YAML, and DB files.
  • Restore /opt/compose separately, because this Offen archive was appdata-only.
  • Decide whether to rebuild compose files manually or from other backups.
  • Consider a safer future backup format:
  • archive-first locally
  • then transfer the archive to NAS

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Always confirm the exact archive type and internal path structure before restoring.
  • Shell variables are lost after logout; long restore workflows should be rerun from a fresh, self-contained set of commands.
  • For large SMB-backed restores, copying the archive locally first is more reliable than extracting directly from the mounted share.
  • Offen appdata backups do not automatically solve missing compose files.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: qm set 101 -scsi1 cephpool:vm-100-disk-0

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Attaches VM 100’s Ceph-backed disk to helper VM 101 as an extra SCSI disk.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• qm set: Modify a Proxmox VM config.
  • 101: Helper VM ID.
  • -scsi1: Attach the disk as the second SCSI device.
  • cephpool:vm-100-disk-0: The existing VM disk on Ceph storage.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To mount and repair VM 100 offline from a helper VM.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: VM 101 gains visibility of VM 100’s disk.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: the disk appears in helper VM block devices.
  • Failure: wrong storage backend or wrong disk identifier was used.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Attaching the wrong disk can risk modifying the wrong VM’s data.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Double-check with Proxmox VM config and storage listing before attaching.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: lsblk -o NAME,SIZE,FSTYPE,TYPE,MOUNTPOINT

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Lists block devices, partitions, filesystems, and mountpoints.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • -o: Select output columns.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To identify which device and partition belonged to the offline VM root disk.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A readable map of disks and partitions.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: the correct root partition can be identified.
  • Failure: attached disk missing or helper VM does not see the disk.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: None needed; this is a standard inspection command.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/vm100
for d in dev proc sys run; do sudo mount --bind /$d /mnt/vm100/$d; done
sudo chroot /mnt/vm100 /bin/bash

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Mounts the offline guest root filesystem, bind-mounts critical pseudo-filesystems, and enters a chroot.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• mount --bind: Makes host pseudo-filesystems available inside the chroot.
  • chroot: Changes the apparent root directory of the current shell.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To repair VM 100 as if logged into its own root filesystem.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A root shell inside the mounted guest filesystem.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: admin tools and configuration files can be edited in place.
  • Failure: wrong partition mounted or missing bind mounts.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: High. This directly edits another system’s root filesystem.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Mount read-only first for inspection, then remount read-write only when ready.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: export PATH=/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Sets a full administrative command path inside the current shell.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: No flags.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: The rescue shell or chroot session did not have admin tools in the path.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Commands such as useradd, groupadd, and chpasswd become available.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: admin commands can be executed.
  • Failure: binaries may be missing from the chrooted system.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
rm -f /etc/nologin || true
getent group sudo >/dev/null || groupadd sudo

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Removes a file that blocks logins and ensures the sudo group exists.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -f: Ignore missing file.
  • || true: Avoid aborting on benign failure.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To restore login access in the recovered VM.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: No login-block file and a valid sudo group.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: local login barriers are removed.
  • Failure: filesystem or chroot issues remain.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
id -u rescue >/dev/null 2>&1 || /usr/sbin/useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G sudo rescue
echo 'rescue:Docker123' | /usr/sbin/chpasswd

id -u debian >/dev/null 2>&1 || /usr/sbin/useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G sudo debian
echo 'debian:Docker123' | /usr/sbin/chpasswd

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Creates or resets local users and passwords.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• useradd -m: Create a home directory.
  • -s /bin/bash: Set shell.
  • -G sudo: Add user to the sudo group.
  • chpasswd: Set password from stdin.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To restore console and admin access.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Working local credentials for recovery.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: passwords reset and user accounts exist.
  • Failure: broken passwd/shadow state, bad chroot, or missing tools.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Password resets directly affect guest authentication.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sed -i -E 's/^\s*#?\s*PasswordAuthentication\s+.*/PasswordAuthentication yes/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sed -i -E 's/^\s*#?\s*(KbdInteractiveAuthentication|ChallengeResponseAuthentication)\s+.*/KbdInteractiveAuthentication yes/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
/usr/sbin/sshd -t

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Enables SSH password-based authentication settings and validates the SSH daemon config.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• sed -i: Edit file in place.
  • -E: Extended regex.
  • sshd -t: Syntax check only.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To repair password-auth SSH access after login problems.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A valid SSH config allowing password auth.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: sshd -t exits cleanly.
  • Failure: config file corruption or bad syntax remains.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. A bad SSH config can lock out remote access.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Back up sshd_config before editing.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
hostnamectl
cat /etc/hostname
sed -n '1,40p' /etc/hosts

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Inspects hostname state and static host mappings.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • sed -n '1,40p': Print the first 40 lines.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To understand why VM 100 appeared as helper-vm.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The current static hostname and local host configuration are displayed.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: hostname drift is visible.
  • Failure: deeper system corruption or missing tools.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo journalctl -b | grep -Ei 'set hostname|cloud-ini|dhcp|dhclient|networkd.*hostname'
systemctl is-enabled cloud-init || true
cloud-init status || true
grep -i preserve_hostname /etc/cloud/cloud.cfg || true
ls -1 /etc/cloud/cloud.cfg.d/ || true

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks whether cloud-init or networking changed the hostname during boot.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• journalctl -b: Current boot logs.
  • grep -Ei: Case-insensitive extended matching.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To confirm whether cloud-init was still controlling hostname behavior.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Evidence of cloud-init activity and relevant settings.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: root cause of hostname drift becomes visible.
  • Failure: incomplete logs or missing cloud-init.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
findmnt /var/lib/docker /var/lib/docker/appdata /var/lib/docker/compose || true
grep -Ev '^\s*#|^\s*$' /etc/fstab | sed -n '1,200p'
systemctl status docker-verify.mountguard.service --no-pager || true
systemctl status docker --no-pager || true

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks actual mount state, active fstab entries, and Docker-related systemd unit status.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• findmnt: Shows real mount relationships.
  • grep -Ev: Filters out comments and blank lines.
  • --no-pager: Print full unit status directly.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To diagnose why Docker root and bind mounts were inconsistent.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A clear picture of whether /var/lib/docker is really mounted and whether Docker guard logic is behaving.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: mount state becomes clear.
  • Failure: path confusion persists or units are broken.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo mount -a
findmnt /var/lib/docker

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Applies all fstab mounts and then confirms whether Docker root is mounted.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • mount -a: Mount all eligible entries from fstab.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To apply repaired mount definitions.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: /var/lib/docker appears as a real mountpoint if fstab is correct.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: dedicated Docker data mount is active.
  • Failure: fstab entry wrong, device missing, or mount failed.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Bad fstab entries can cause boot or mount issues.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo bash -lc 'touch /opt/compose/.bindtest && ls -la /var/lib/docker/compose | grep bindtest || echo "compose bind not active"'
sudo bash -lc 'touch /opt/docker-apps/.bindtest && ls -la /var/lib/docker/appdata | grep bindtest || echo "appdata bind not active"'

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Creates a test file on one side of the bind and checks whether it appears on the other side.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• bash -lc: Run a login-like shell command string.
  • touch: Create a file if missing.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To prove whether the bind mounts were actually working.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The test file appears on the opposite path.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: real bind relationship exists.
  • Failure: bind not active or path direction wrong.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo install -m 600 -o root -g root /dev/null /root/.smbcredentials
sudo bash -lc 'cat > /root/.smbcredentials <<EOF
username=admin
password=Kingsley1
# domain=WORKGROUP
EOF'

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Creates a protected SMB credentials file.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• install -m 600: Create file with strict permissions.
  • -o root -g root: Root ownership.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: CIFS mounts needed credentials without exposing them in command history or fstab.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A root-readable-only credentials file.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: CIFS mounts can authenticate.
  • Failure: mount auth errors continue.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: High. Contains plaintext credentials.

docs.title: Safer alternative
docs.text: Use a separate secret-management method if available.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo mount -t cifs //192.168.16.21/Media /srv/remotemount/NAS \
  -o rw,vers=3.1.1,credentials=/root/.smbcredentials,uid=1000,gid=1000,iocharset=utf8,mfsymlinks,noperm,noserverino,dir_mode=0775,file_mode=0664,actimeo=1,cache=none

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Mounts the NAS share manually over SMB/CIFS.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• vers=3.1.1: SMB protocol version.
  • credentials=...: Use stored SMB credentials.
  • uid/gid=1000: Present files as owned by the Debian user.
  • mfsymlinks: Emulate Linux symlinks on SMB.
  • cache=none: Minimize caching for correctness.
  • actimeo=1: Keep attribute cache lifetime short.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To validate a working mount before committing the config to fstab.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: A working NAS mount with file ownership and symlink behavior matching Linux expectations.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: NAS is accessible and Linux workflows work.
  • Failure: credentials, share path, or SMB options are wrong.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. A bad mount command can mask path issues or hang on network problems.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
ln -s target.txt /srv/remotemount/NAS/_cifs_test/link-to-target
readlink /srv/remotemount/NAS/_cifs_test/link-to-target
cat /srv/remotemount/NAS/_cifs_test/link-to-target

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Creates and tests a symlink on the mounted NAS share.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• ln -s: Create symbolic link.
  • readlink: Show link target.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To prove mfsymlinks solved the SMB symlink problem.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The symlink resolves and file contents are readable through it.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: SMB symlink emulation works.
  • Failure: mount options are insufficient.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
systemctl list-units 'srv-remotemount-NAS.*' --all
systemctl status srv-remotemount-NAS.automount srv-remotemount-NAS.mount

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Checks the fstab-generated systemd automount and mount units.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• --all: Include inactive units.
  • status: Show current unit state and logs.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To validate the automount behavior after fixing /etc/fstab.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: The automount unit is active and the mount unit activates on access.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: the NAS mount is persistent and on-demand.
  • Failure: fstab or systemd generator input is still wrong.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
rsync -aHAX --delete --numeric-ids "$APPDATA_SRC"/ "$DEST_APP"/
rsync -aHAX --delete --numeric-ids "$COMPOSE_SRC"/ "$DEST_CMP"/

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Copies appdata and compose trees to backup destinations while preserving metadata.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -a: Archive mode.
  • -H: Preserve hard links.
  • -A: Preserve ACLs.
  • -X: Preserve extended attributes.
  • --delete: Remove files from destination not present in source.
  • --numeric-ids: Preserve numeric ownership.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To create restorable NAS snapshots of Docker state.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Backup snapshot directories matching the source tree.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: backup snapshots are usable for restore.
  • Failure: SMB or permission issues interrupt the copy.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. --delete can erase destination content if paths are reversed.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
gzip -t "$TMP/archive.tar.gz" && echo "gzip OK"
tar -tzf "$TMP/archive.tar.gz" | head -20

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Tests archive integrity and lists the first few archive entries.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• gzip -t: Validate gzip compression layer.
  • tar -tzf: List contents of a gzip-compressed tar archive.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: To confirm the Offen backup archive was valid and to inspect its internal path layout.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: gzip OK and visible archive entries.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: archive is readable and path structure is known.
  • Failure: corrupted archive or wrong file format.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Low.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
sudo tar -xzpf "$TMP/archive.tar.gz" \
  -C "$DEST" \
  --strip-components=2 \
  /backup/my-app-backup

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Extracts the backup payload directly into the appdata destination while discarding wrapper path components.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text:
• -x: Extract.
  • -z: Gzip-compressed archive.
  • -p: Preserve permissions.
  • -f: Archive file.
  • -C "$DEST": Extract into destination directory.
  • --strip-components=2: Remove the first two path components.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: The archive root was /backup/my-app-backup/..., but the desired restore target was /opt/docker-apps/....

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: App directories are restored directly into /opt/docker-apps.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: payload lands at the desired depth.
  • Failure: wrong archive path or wrong strip depth.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: High. A bad strip value can place files into the wrong location.

docs.title: Command
docs.text: sudo rsync -a --remove-source-files /opt/docker-apps/backup/my-app-backup/ /opt/docker-apps/

docs.title: What it does
docs.text: Moves restored contents out of an accidentally nested wrapper directory into the real appdata root.

docs.title: Important flags or arguments
docs.text: • --remove-source-files: Delete source files after successful copy.

docs.title: Why it was used
docs.text: An earlier restore left the payload nested under /opt/docker-apps/backup/my-app-backup.

docs.title: Expected result
docs.text: Files move up into the correct root while the nested wrapper becomes empty.

docs.title: Success or failure meaning
docs.text:
• Success: restored tree ends up in the expected location.
  • Failure: source and destination remain inconsistent.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Moderate. Using remove-source semantics is risky if source and destination are wrong.

docs.title: Command Reference
docs.text: Quick lookup for common infrastructure, cloud, automation, security, and troubleshooting commands.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Replace anything in <angle-brackets> before running.
  • Review destructive commands carefully.
  • Prefer check commands before change commands.

docs.title: Proxmox
docs.text:
# List VMs
qm list

# Show VM config
qm config <vmid>

# Rebuild cloud-init ISO
qm cloudinit update <vmid>

docs.title: Ceph
docs.text:
# Check Ceph health
ceph -s

# Show detailed health
ceph health detail

# Show OSD tree
ceph osd tree

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
# List running containers
docker ps

# Show Compose services
docker compose ps

# Show container logs
docker logs <container-name>

docs.title: Date Rule
docs.text:
Remove dates from filenames, visible headings, indexes, generated metadata, and runbook body content.

Use logical event order instead of date order.

docs.title: Incident Format
docs.text:
# Short Incident Title

## Summary
Briefly explain what happened and why it mattered.

## Environment
List relevant systems, services, hosts, VMs, containers, storage, and network details.

## Problem
Describe symptoms, errors, and impact.

## Timeline
Document the sequence of events without relying on dates.

## Investigation
Describe what was checked and what was found.

## Resolution
Explain what fixed the issue.

## Validation
Show how the fix was confirmed.

## Lessons Learned
Capture reusable takeaways.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Add common terms, acronyms, and homelab-specific naming here.

docs.title: Main Rule
docs.text:
Did this happen in the real homelab?
  yes -> infrastructure/
  no  -> cloud/

docs.title: Date Rule
docs.text:
Remove dates from:

  • filenames
  • visible headings
  • indexes
  • generated metadata
  • runbook body content

Use logical event order instead of date order.

docs.title: Ordering Rule
docs.text:
Order documents by logical event flow inside each category.

design/planning -> baseline setup -> configuration -> incident -> recovery -> hardening -> follow-up

docs.title: Recurring Patterns
docs.text: Capture repeated troubleshooting patterns and reusable operational lessons here.
